events.title: THE IMPOUNDED TANKER
events.description: An Iranian Revolutionary Guard fast-boat swarm harassed a commercial tanker three days ago. The crew is safe but the ship is impounded in Bandar Abbas. Your predecessor resigned in disgrace. You are the replacement. Nobody expects you to last.

choices.label: Demand release through official channels
choices.flavor: You follow process. The world sees restraint. Iran sees an opening.
choices.consequence: You pick up the secure phone and dial the Iranian mission in Geneva. The room watches you work the diplomatic machinery — patient, methodical. Admiral Chen exchanges a look with his deputy. Iran's charge d'affaires listens politely, promises to 'convey your concerns.' The tanker sits in Bandar Abbas. The world sees restraint. Tehran sees something else entirely.

choices.label: Send a destroyer to loiter outside Bandar Abbas
choices.flavor: You say nothing. The image speaks. IRGC commanders take note.
choices.consequence: You say nothing. You simply nod at Admiral Chen. Within the hour, the USS Arleigh Burke alters course for Bandar Abbas, her radar arrays painting the Iranian coastline. No statement is issued. No calls are made. The image of an American destroyer loitering twelve nautical miles off an Iranian port speaks volumes. IRGC commanders begin making phone calls.

choices.label: Call the flag-state directly — propose a joint response
choices.flavor: Shared burden, shared credit. The coalition option opens.
choices.consequence: You reach for the phone, but not the one to Tehran. You call the Liberian maritime authority directly and propose a joint response — shared burden, shared optics. The flag-state ambassador is surprised, then intrigued. The coalition seed is planted before Iran can frame this as a bilateral confrontation.

characterFlavor.trump: You glance at the TV monitors lining the wall — every network is running the seizure footage on loop. This is the kind of crisis that makes or breaks a presidency on the six o'clock news.
characterFlavor.hegseth: You study the tactical display with a professional's eye — six fast-boats in a coordinated swarm pattern means someone planned this, trained for it, and is watching to see what you do next.
characterFlavor.kushner: Your phone buzzes — MBS, then MBZ, then Erdogan. Every Gulf leader wants reassurance, and reassurance is a currency you can spend.
characterFlavor.fuentes: Six thousand miles from American soil, an American ship sits under armed guard because fifty years of interventionist policy put it there.
characterFlavor.asmongold: Chat is already pulling satellite imagery and cross-referencing IRGC patrol schedules. Your crowd-sourced intel feed is three hours ahead of the CIA briefing.

events.scene: The Situation Room smells like stale coffee and carpet cleaner. You are three hours into your new assignment, and the wall of monitors already shows your first crisis: a grainy feed from Bandar Abbas harbor, where the MV Stellar Fortune sits under armed guard, her crew confined below decks. Admiral Chen taps the screen with a laser pointer. 'IRGC fast-boat swarm, sir. Six boats, coordinated approach, textbook harassment pattern.' Your predecessor's resignation letter is still warm in the shredder. The Joint Chiefs are sizing you up. Nobody in this room expects you to last the week.

events.title: THE PREDECESSOR'S GHOST
events.description: A classified folder sits on your desk — your predecessor's secret negotiations with Tehran. Reading it is knowledge. Burning it is a clean start. The contents are radioactive either way.

choices.label: Read the classified file
choices.flavor: You now know Iran's real red line. This knowledge will matter — if it doesn't destroy you first.
choices.consequence: You break the seal. The pages reveal a secret channel through Oman, three rounds of preliminary talks, and a document that maps Iran's actual red lines — not the ones they broadcast on state TV. Your predecessor was closer to a deal than anyone knew. The knowledge settles into your chest like a stone. You now understand Iran's real breaking point. Whether this saves you or destroys you depends on what you do next.

choices.label: Burn the file — start clean
choices.flavor: The public trusts a clean break. But you'll discover Iran's red line the hard way — through a crisis.
choices.consequence: You pick up the folder, walk to the fireplace in the private study, and feed it to the flames. The paper curls, blackens, and disappears. Your chief of staff watches from the doorway. 'Clean start,' you tell him. He nods slowly. The public will trust a leader unburdened by backroom deals. But somewhere in Tehran, there is a red line you will now have to discover the hard way — through a crisis.

events.scene: The classified folder sits on the mahogany desk, sealed with red tape and a 'NOFORN' stamp. Your predecessor's chief of staff left it here deliberately — a parting gift or a poisoned chalice. The air conditioning hums. Through the reinforced windows, you can see the Rose Garden, impossibly green against the gray sky. Your hand hovers over the folder. The paper inside details secret negotiations with Tehran that never made the news. Your aide stands at the door, waiting. 'Sir, the morning brief starts in ten minutes.' The contents are radioactive either way — but so is ignorance.

events.title: THE ADMIRAL'S WARNING
events.description: Your first real briefing. Admiral Chen stands at the podium with satellite imagery of Iranian naval movements. "We need to increase patrol tempo immediately," he says. Your civilian advisors shift uncomfortably.

choices.label: Defer to the Admiral — increase patrols
choices.flavor: The Admiral nods. Your civilian advisors resent being sidelined. The Pentagon respects the energy.
choices.consequence: You nod. 'Do it, Admiral.' Chen closes his folder with quiet satisfaction. Three additional destroyer rotations are scheduled within the hour. Your civilian advisors file out in silence, resentment simmering beneath their professional composure. At the Pentagon, the word spreads: the new boss listens to the uniforms. In Tehran, satellite analysts note the increased tempo and begin their own calculations.

choices.label: Convene your full team before deciding
choices.flavor: The Admiral respects the process. Barely. Your team appreciates being heard.
choices.consequence: You raise a hand. 'I want my full team in the room before we change our posture.' Admiral Chen pauses, then nods — slowly, grudgingly. He respects the process, barely. Your national security team assembles for a two-hour session that produces a measured response. Everyone feels heard. The decision comes later, more informed but less decisive.

choices.label: Override — propose a diplomatic signal instead
choices.flavor: The Admiral begins quietly briefing Congress about your "lack of seriousness." But Araghchi notices.
choices.consequence: You lean forward. 'Admiral, what if we sent a different kind of signal?' The room goes quiet. You outline a diplomatic overture — direct message to Tehran through Swiss intermediaries. Chen's jaw tightens. He begins composing a memo to the Armed Services Committee chairman about your 'lack of seriousness.' But six thousand miles away, Iranian Foreign Minister Araghchi reads the signal and picks up his own phone.

events.scene: The Pentagon briefing room is windowless, lit by fluorescent panels that give everyone the complexion of the dead. Admiral Chen stands at the podium with a clicker in one hand and thirty years of naval warfare behind his eyes. The satellite imagery on the screen shows Iranian naval movements — Bandar Abbas, Jask, Qeshm Island — in sharp resolution. 'We need to increase patrol tempo immediately,' he says, his voice flat and certain. Behind you, your civilian advisors shift in their leather chairs. The Secretary of State clears her throat. The room divides along a fault line as old as the republic: military necessity versus diplomatic caution.

events.title: THE JOURNALIST'S CALL
events.description: A New York Times reporter is on the line. She's working on a piece about your first 72 hours. Off-the-record, she says. But you know how that goes.

choices.label: Give an off-the-record briefing
choices.flavor: You shape the narrative. But there's a 20% chance she burns you.
choices.consequence: You take the call. For twenty minutes, you shape the narrative — measured, competent, aware of the stakes. Watkins asks probing questions but you stay ahead of her. The piece runs three days later: 'New Crisis Manager Brings Steady Hand to Strait Standoff.' But buried in paragraph sixteen is a detail you don't remember sharing. A 20% chance she burns you on the next one.

choices.label: Written statement through press office
choices.flavor: "Administration stonewalling" runs above the fold. Safe but unhelpful.
choices.consequence: You dictate a written statement through your press secretary. Four paragraphs of careful nothing. Watkins runs her story anyway: 'New Strait Manager Stonewalls Press on First Day.' It's above the fold, framed by a photo that makes you look evasive. Safe, but the narrative is now hers to control.

choices.label: "No comment" — hang up
choices.flavor: She becomes hostile. Future media events will be harder.
choices.consequence: You tell your assistant: 'No comment. And don't put her through again.' The line goes dead. Watkins sits in her Manhattan office, staring at her phone. She crosses your name off the cooperative list and adds it to another list entirely. Future media engagements just got significantly harder.

events.scene: Your assistant appears in the doorway with that particular expression that means trouble. 'Emily Watkins, New York Times. She says it's urgent.' The name lands like a small grenade. Watkins broke the Abu Ghraib story. She exposed the NSA domestic surveillance program. She has a Pulitzer and the phone number of every disgruntled staffer in Washington. Now she wants to talk about your first seventy-two hours. 'Off the record,' she promises through the receiver. But you can hear her keyboard clicking in the background. The recorder is already running. It always is.

events.title: ALLY ON THE LINE: THE BRITISH PM
events.description: The British Prime Minister is offering a joint naval task force. Shared command, shared rules of engagement. It's generous — suspiciously so. Nothing is free in the special relationship.

choices.label: Accept — joint task force
choices.flavor: Shared command means shared veto. You lose unilateral strike capability. But the coalition is real.
choices.consequence: You accept. The joint task force is announced within hours — a press conference at 10 Downing Street, a handshake between admirals. The coalition is real. But shared command means shared veto. You have just traded unilateral strike capability for the legitimacy of a partner. In Tehran, analysts note the development and begin calculating how to drive a wedge between London and Washington.

choices.label: Counter with parallel operations — same area, separate commands
choices.flavor: You keep your freedom. The UK is mildly insulted but professional.
choices.consequence: You counter-propose: parallel operations in the same area, separate command structures, shared intelligence. The PM goes quiet for a moment. 'Rather defeats the purpose, doesn't it?' he says, mildly. The UK is professionally insulted but too disciplined to show it. You keep your freedom of action. The coalition is thinner but real.

choices.label: Ask what they want in return
choices.flavor: The PM wants your vote on a UN trade resolution. Opens a side-negotiation.
choices.consequence: You lean back in your chair. 'What do you want in return?' The PM laughs — that particular English laugh that means you've said the quiet part out loud. He mentions the UN trade resolution. A side negotiation opens, and suddenly the strait crisis has become a chessboard with pieces you didn't know were in play.

events.scene: The call comes in on the encrypted line at 6:47 AM, before your first coffee has cooled. The British Prime Minister's voice carries the particular warmth of someone who wants something. He proposes a joint naval task force — shared command structure, shared rules of engagement, Royal Navy vessels integrated with the Fifth Fleet. It is generous. It is suspiciously generous. Through the Situation Room window, the first light catches the Washington Monument. Your national security advisor slides a note across the desk: 'UK wants our vote on the UN trade resolution. This is the price.' Nothing is free in the special relationship. It never has been.

events.title: UN SECURITY COUNCIL SHOWDOWN
events.description: The Security Council is in emergency session. You have one shot to get a binding resolution. Russia is leaning veto. China is undecided. Your ambassador is waiting for instructions.

choices.label: Push for binding resolution with sanctions
choices.flavor: Russia abstains. China follows. The resolution passes. Iran is formally isolated.
choices.consequence: You push for the binding resolution with full sanctions. The vote proceeds in alphabetical order. Your heart hammers with each 'aye.' Russia — abstains. A murmur ripples through the chamber. China follows suit. The resolution passes. Iran is formally, legally, internationally isolated. Your ambassador permits herself the ghost of a smile.

choices.label: Non-binding statement of concern — play it safe
choices.flavor: Safe, symbolic, useless. Your team is demoralized.
choices.consequence: You instruct your ambassador to support the non-binding statement of concern. It passes unanimously — fifteen hands raised in a gesture that means precisely nothing. In the hallway afterward, a European diplomat mutters, 'Well, that was symbolic.' Your team flies home demoralized. You had leverage, and you spent it on air.

choices.label: Dramatic intelligence presentation — name IRGC commanders
choices.flavor: The room is shocked. Iran scrambles. But you just burned intelligence sources.
choices.consequence: Your ambassador stands and requests the floor. 'I would like to present classified intelligence to this body.' The chamber goes silent. Satellite photos appear on the screens — IRGC commanders identified by name, their operations mapped in granular detail. The Iranian delegation scrambles for their phones. The room is shocked. But in Langley, the Director of the CIA is watching with a clenched jaw. You just burned intelligence sources that took years to develop.

events.scene: The Security Council chamber is a theatre of controlled fury. Your ambassador sits in the American chair, earpiece in, waiting for your instructions relayed from six thousand miles away. Russia's ambassador is leaning back with studied indifference — but his aides are passing notes furiously. China's representative stares at the draft resolution, her pen hovering. The gallery is packed with journalists. The Iranian delegation sits stone-faced in the observer section. You have one shot at a binding resolution. One shot to make the international community put its name on paper. The clock on the wall reads 3:47 PM in New York, and the President of the Security Council is about to open the floor.

events.title: PROXY IGNITION: HOUTHI STRIKE
events.description: Houthis launched anti-ship missiles at a commercial vessel in the Red Sea. The crisis is no longer contained to the strait — it's metastasizing. Your budget is burning and allies are nervous.

choices.label: Strike the Houthi launch site
choices.flavor: Proportional. Professional. Saudi Arabia is grateful. But you just opened a second front.
choices.consequence: You authorize the strike. Tomahawk missiles arc from the USS Gravely toward the Houthi launch site in western Yemen. The facility is destroyed — clean, proportional, professional. In Riyadh, the Saudi Defense Minister calls to express personal gratitude. But you have just opened a second front, and the budget line for 'kinetic operations' doubles overnight.

choices.label: Intercept missiles — let proxies tire themselves out
choices.flavor: Defense only. The Houthis try again in 3 days.
choices.consequence: You choose defense over offense. Interceptor missiles destroy the Houthi weapons in flight, their fragments splashing harmlessly into the Red Sea. The tanker sails on, unharmed. Your admirals report the intercept as a success. But the Houthis still have their launchers, their supply chain, their will. They will try again in three days.

choices.label: Tell Iran through channels: proxy attacks = Tehran's responsibility
choices.flavor: A red line. If Iran believes you, this changes everything. If they don't, it changes nothing.
choices.consequence: You pick up the phone to the Omani back-channel. The message to Tehran is simple and clear: proxy attacks will be treated as Iranian attacks. A red line, drawn in language that cannot be misunderstood. In Tehran, the message lands on two desks simultaneously — Araghchi's and Tangsiri's. If Araghchi believes you, the calculus changes. If Tangsiri doesn't, it changes nothing.

events.scene: The alert comes from CENTCOM's Red Sea desk: Houthi anti-ship missiles launched from the Yemeni coast, tracking toward a commercial vessel in the Bab el-Mandeb strait. The missile tracks appear on the tactical display as angry red arcs. The crisis, which you had carefully contained within the Strait of Hormuz, is metastasizing. Your budget officer slides a revised expenditure report across the table — the numbers are bleeding red. An aide whispers that the French ambassador has requested an urgent call. The map on the wall now shows two active theaters, and Iran's proxy network is waking up like a nervous system sensing pain.

events.title: THE CONGRESSIONAL HEARING
events.description: Senate Armed Services Committee. Live on C-SPAN. The chairman opens with: "The American people deserve to know why we're spending $50 million a day on a crisis that's getting worse." The cameras are rolling.

choices.label: Testify personally — face the music
choices.flavor: You came prepared. The viral clip is you calmly dismantling the chairman's argument with classified data.
choices.consequence: You testify personally. For three hours, you face every question — hostile, sympathetic, and grandstanding alike. You came prepared, and it shows. The viral clip comes at the two-hour mark: you calmly dismantle the chairman's argument with a single declassified data point that makes him blink. 'Senator, with respect, your numbers are from 2019. Here are the current figures.' The room shifts. By evening, your approval ticks up three points.

choices.label: Send your deputy — you're managing a crisis
choices.flavor: Congress is insulted. Future funding requests just got harder.
choices.consequence: You send your deputy. The official reason is 'ongoing crisis management demands the coordinator's presence.' The real reason is that you cannot afford to be pinned down on camera for four hours. Congress is insulted — deeply, professionally insulted. The chairman's closing statement includes the phrase 'contempt for this body's oversight role.' Future funding requests just became significantly harder.

choices.label: Testify AND declassify satellite imagery live on camera
choices.flavor: The room gasps. Iran scrambles. The Pentagon is furious about the declassification. But the public is with you.
choices.consequence: You testify, and then you do something unprecedented: you declassify satellite imagery live on camera. The room gasps as the screen shows Iranian mine-laying operations in crystal clarity. The chairman's mouth opens, then closes. The Pentagon is furious about the unauthorized declassification. But the public — the public is with you, and the footage is on every screen in America by dinner.

characterFlavor.trump: The hearing room cameras are live on C-SPAN and every cable network. Three hours of free airtime — more than most candidates get in an entire primary season.
characterFlavor.hegseth: The senators want political theater; you brought operational data. Every chart, every satellite image, every casualty number is a fact that cuts through the grandstanding.
characterFlavor.kushner: You scan the committee members' faces, calculating which ones have donors connected to your Gulf contacts. Congressional hearings are about relationships, not testimony.
characterFlavor.fuentes: The chairman opens with spending numbers designed to make you look reckless. He voted for the Iraq War, the Libya intervention, and the Syria operation. Your base remembers, even if the cameras don't show it.
characterFlavor.asmongold: You're streaming the hearing prep live — chat is crowdsourcing rebuttals to the committee's likely questions. Viewer count just hit a new high. This is S-tier content.

events.scene: The hearing room in the Hart Senate Office Building is packed to the walls. Camera operators jostle for angles. The Senate Armed Services Committee chairman, a silver-haired veteran of three wars, peers down from the raised dais and opens with the line everyone will remember: 'The American people deserve to know why we're spending fifty million dollars a day on a crisis that's getting worse.' The C-SPAN cameras capture every bead of sweat, every shuffle of paper. Your legal counsel sits behind you, a stack of classified briefing cards ready. The gallery is full of reporters, lobbyists, and two families who lost sons in the strait.

events.title: IRAN'S ULTIMATUM
events.description: Mojtaba Khamenei appears on state television: "Lift sanctions on our central bank within 72 hours, or the next vessel we seize will fly the American flag." Tangsiri stands behind him, arms crossed. The clock is ticking.

choices.label: Call the bluff publicly — "America does not respond to threats"
choices.flavor: If Iran follows through, this gets catastrophic. If they back down, you win the crisis.
choices.consequence: You walk to the podium in the White House press room. The cameras flash. Your voice is steady: 'America does not respond to threats. We will not be blackmailed. Period.' The room erupts with questions. In Tehran, the broadcast cuts to Tangsiri's face — he is smiling. If Iran follows through, you own the catastrophe. If they back down, you own the crisis. The next seventy-two hours will determine which.

choices.label: Counter-propose through channels — mutual step-down
choices.flavor: 50% Iran accepts. Araghchi is pushing for it internally. Tangsiri wants blood.
choices.consequence: You reach for the Omani back-channel. Your message is precise: mutual step-down, simultaneous gestures, no public humiliation for either side. Fifty-fifty odds, your intelligence people say. Araghchi is pushing for it internally — he sees the off-ramp. But Tangsiri wants blood, and the IRGC has its own chain of command. The counter-proposal travels through three intermediaries and lands on a desk in Tehran.

choices.label: Prepare quietly — move assets, brief allies, say nothing
choices.flavor: Iran is uncertain. Their own internal debate intensifies. Sometimes silence is the loudest response.
choices.consequence: You do nothing visible. No statement, no press conference, no tweets. But beneath the surface, assets move: a carrier group repositions, allies are briefed on secure lines, submarine patrols intensify. Iran's intelligence apparatus watches and reports back conflicting assessments. Sometimes silence is the loudest response of all.

characterFlavor.trump: Mojtaba's face fills the screen, but you're already thinking about your response — which camera angle, which phrase will dominate the news cycle for the next seventy-two hours.
characterFlavor.hegseth: You assess the ultimatum through a military lens — is the IRGC positioned to execute, or is this political theater backed by insufficient force?
characterFlavor.kushner: An ultimatum is an opening bid dressed up as a final offer. You've seen this in a hundred real estate negotiations — the question is what they'll actually settle for.
characterFlavor.fuentes: Iran issued an ultimatum because American sanctions gave them nothing left to lose. The establishment's maximum pressure policy created the very crisis they're now asking you to solve with more military spending.
characterFlavor.asmongold: Seventy-two hour countdown timer on screen. Chat is already running probability models. This is the moment that proves whether your threat analysis was right or completely off.

events.scene: Iranian state television fills the Situation Room's main screen. Mojtaba Khamenei stands at a podium flanked by IRGC flags, his father's portrait looming behind him. His voice is measured but his eyes are cold: 'Lift sanctions on our central bank within seventy-two hours, or the next vessel we seize will fly the American flag.' Behind him, Admiral Tangsiri stands with his arms crossed, the ghost of a smile on his lips. The room goes silent. Your watch officer stops writing. The clock on the wall becomes the loudest sound in the room. Seventy-two hours. Three days to decide whether this is a bluff you can call or a promise you cannot afford to test.

events.title: THE JOURNALIST RETURNS
events.description: Remember the Times reporter? She's back. This time she wants to be embedded with your team for 48 hours. Radical transparency — or radical exposure. Your relationship with her determines how this plays out.

choices.label: Full on-the-record interview — you've earned it
choices.flavor: Your narrative is coherent. The piece is fair. For once, the press is on your side.
choices.consequence: You give her the full on-the-record interview. Ninety minutes in your office, no ground rules, no pre-screened questions. Your narrative is coherent because your strategy is coherent, and it shows. The piece runs as a Sunday magazine cover: 'The Steady Hand.' For the first time in weeks, the press is on your side.

choices.label: Embed her with your team for 48 hours
choices.flavor: She sees everything — including your mistakes. But the transparency is powerful. 70% chance of a glowing profile.
choices.consequence: You let her in. For forty-eight hours, Watkins shadows your team through briefings, calls, and the small human moments between crises. She sees the mistakes — the 3 AM wrong call, the briefing card you read upside down. But she also sees the competence, the dedication, the human cost. There is a seventy percent chance the resulting profile is glowing. There is a thirty percent chance it ends your career.

choices.label: Feed her a specific story you want published
choices.flavor: It works — this time. But journalists have long memories about being used.
choices.consequence: You feed her a specific story — a carefully constructed narrative about the back-channel progress, designed to pressure Iran through public opinion. Watkins runs it exactly as you intended. It works beautifully, this time. But journalists have institutional memory about being used as instruments of policy, and Emily Watkins has the longest memory in Washington.

events.scene: Emily Watkins is back, and this time she is not asking for quotes. She wants to be embedded with your team for forty-eight hours — 'radical transparency,' she calls it, leaning forward in the visitor's chair with the kind of intensity that wins Pulitzers. Your press secretary stands behind her, making throat-cutting gestures. But Watkins has been fair so far, and the public narrative needs shaping. Through the window, you can see the cable news trucks multiplying on the North Lawn. The story is being written with or without your cooperation. The only question is whether you hold the pen.

events.title: CONGRESSIONAL HEARING
events.description: Senate Armed Services Committee demands testimony. They threaten to cut your budget.

choices.label: Justify with intelligence
choices.flavor: You declassify key intel. Congress is satisfied — for now.
choices.consequence: You open the classified folder. Satellite imagery, intercepted communications, the IRGC order of battle — all of it laid before the committee in a secure session. The senators lean forward. The chairman's hostility softens into something approaching concern. 'Why weren't we briefed sooner?' he asks. Congress is satisfied, for now. But the intelligence community notices that their crown jewels were used to win a budget fight.

choices.label: Promise to cut spending
choices.flavor: Congress relents but expects follow-through.
choices.consequence: You promise to cut spending. The words taste like compromise, but you deliver them with conviction. 'We will reduce daily operational costs by twenty percent within two weeks.' Congress relents, the budget survives, and your staff begins the grim work of deciding which patrols to cancel and which intelligence assets to mothball.

choices.label: Stonewall the committee
choices.flavor: Refusing backfires. Media coverage turns hostile.
choices.consequence: You stonewall the committee. 'The operational details of this mission are classified for the safety of our personnel.' The chairman's face hardens. Three senators make statements to the press afterward, each more hostile than the last. By evening, the media narrative has shifted from the strait to your 'arrogance and contempt for oversight.'

events.scene: The Senate Armed Services Committee hearing room is half-empty but fully hostile. The chairman adjusts his microphone with the practiced slowness of a man who knows the cameras are rolling. 'We have serious concerns about the trajectory of spending,' he begins, shuffling a stack of budget projections. Your budget liaison whispers the number: they are threatening to cut your operational funding by thirty percent. The committee staffers have prepared a damning chart that compares your daily expenditure to the cost of school lunches. It is reductive and effective.

events.title: ALLIED NAVAL REQUEST
events.description: Japan requests dedicated US Navy escort for their tanker fleet. It strengthens the alliance but costs resources.

choices.label: Assign escorts
choices.flavor: Japanese tankers sail with USN escorts. Tokyo publicly thanks Washington.
choices.consequence: You assign the escorts. Within forty-eight hours, Japanese-flagged tankers sail through the strait under the watchful guns of US Navy destroyers. In Tokyo, the Prime Minister makes a public statement of gratitude that leads every Japanese news broadcast. The alliance is strengthened. Your naval commanders wince at the stretched resources but comply without complaint.

choices.label: Decline politely
choices.flavor: Japan is disappointed but understanding.
choices.consequence: You decline, politely and with genuine regret. The ambassador absorbs the news with characteristic composure, but you can see the disappointment in the slight tightening around his eyes. Japan begins quietly exploring alternative supply routes and emergency rationing plans. The alliance endures, but a small crack has appeared in its foundation.

events.scene: The Japanese ambassador arrives at the State Department with a formal request printed on cream-colored paper bearing the chrysanthemum seal. Japan — the world's fourth-largest economy, importer of 88% of its oil through the Strait of Hormuz — is requesting dedicated US Navy escorts for its tanker fleet. The ambassador bows slightly as he presents the document. Behind the diplomatic courtesy, there is real fear. Tokyo's strategic petroleum reserve will last sixty days. After that, the lights begin to go out in the world's most energy-dependent nation.

events.title: HUMANITARIAN CRISIS
events.description: An Iranian fishing vessel capsized near the strait. 40 fishermen in the water. Your destroyer is 15 minutes away.

choices.label: Rescue immediately
choices.flavor: Your sailors pull 38 survivors from the water. The footage goes viral.
choices.consequence: You give the order: rescue immediately. The USS Halsey comes about hard, her wake curving white against the dark water. Fifteen minutes later, American sailors are pulling Iranian fishermen from the sea — thirty-eight survivors hauled over the gunwales, wrapped in blankets, given water. A petty officer holds an old man's hand while a corpsman checks his vitals. The footage, captured by the ship's cameras, goes viral within hours. For one moment, in the midst of a geopolitical crisis, humanity prevails.

choices.label: Coordinate with Iran
choices.flavor: Joint effort saves most of the crew.
choices.consequence: You call the Iranian naval liaison through the deconfliction channel. A joint rescue is coordinated — the USS Halsey provides on-scene coordination while Iranian coast guard vessels handle the recovery. Most of the crew is saved. The cooperation is awkward, tense, and ultimately successful. No one calls it a breakthrough, but somewhere in the machinery of both navies, a small thread of professional respect forms.

choices.label: Stay on mission
choices.flavor: Iranian state media reports US ships watched fishermen drown.
choices.consequence: You order the Halsey to maintain its patrol route. The mission comes first. The fishermen are in Iranian waters; Iran can handle its own. Twelve hours later, Iranian state media broadcasts footage of empty life jackets floating in the strait, with the caption: 'American warships watched as Iranian fishermen drowned.' The footage is devastating. The truth — that your ship was on an active defense patrol — does not matter. The image does.

events.scene: The report comes from your destroyer captain, USS Halsey, patrolling the eastern approach to the strait: an Iranian fishing vessel has capsized in heavy swells three nautical miles south of Qeshm Island. Forty fishermen are in the water. The captain's voice is calm but urgent on the radio — he can see them through his binoculars, heads bobbing in the gray-green waves. Your destroyer is fifteen minutes away at flank speed. Iranian coast guard vessels are forty-five minutes out. The water temperature is survivable for about an hour. The fishermen are not soldiers. They are fathers, brothers, sons. But this is an active naval confrontation zone, and your ship pulling alongside an Iranian vessel creates risks that no rule of engagement fully addresses.

events.title: MEDIA FIRESTORM
events.description: Footage of a near-collision between US and Iranian vessels. Cable news running it 24/7.

choices.label: Release full video
choices.flavor: Unedited footage shows Iranian provocation. Narrative shifts in your favor.
choices.consequence: You release the full, unedited video — all twelve minutes, including the Iranian boat's aggressive approach from two kilometers out. The narrative shifts within hours. Military analysts on CNN point to the Iranian vessel's intercept course. 'This was a deliberate provocation,' says a retired admiral on camera. Your version of events prevails. Iran's state media goes quiet on the subject.

choices.label: Downplay the incident
choices.flavor: The story fades. Both sides quietly move on.
choices.consequence: You downplay the incident. Your press secretary calls it 'a routine encounter that has been sensationalized.' No video release, no finger-pointing. The story fades from the news cycle within forty-eight hours. Both sides quietly move on, and the operational tempo in the strait returns to its tense equilibrium.

choices.label: Blame Iran publicly
choices.flavor: Forceful condemnation rallies base but inflames Iran.
choices.consequence: You go on the offensive. A formal public condemnation, backed by the video evidence, names the IRGC boat commander and demands Iran discipline its forces. Your base rallies. Cable news runs your statement alongside the footage. But in Tehran, the condemnation is played on state TV as evidence of American aggression, and the cycle of provocation accelerates.

events.scene: The footage is ninety seconds of pure chaos: an IRGC fast-boat cutting across the bow of the USS Bataan at what appears to be twenty meters, the American ship sounding its horn, a near-collision that sends spray across both vessels. Someone on the Bataan's bridge screams an obscenity that the microphone catches perfectly. Cable news is running it on a twenty-four-hour loop with the chyron 'NEAR MISS IN THE STRAIT.' The full unedited video, which you have seen, tells a different story — the Iranian boat initiated the provocation by dead-heading toward the Bataan at high speed. But the public only sees the version that sells.

events.title: CHINESE MEDIATION OFFER
events.description: Beijing proposes brokering a deal: they'll pressure Iran if you ease tech restrictions on Chinese firms.

choices.label: Accept the deal
choices.flavor: China delivers. Iran pulls back. But tech concessions draw criticism.
choices.consequence: You accept the deal. Beijing delivers within the week — Iranian fast-boat patrols decrease, a seized tanker is released, and IRGC rhetoric softens noticeably. In Washington, the tech concessions draw sharp criticism from both parties. 'Trading semiconductor sovereignty for temporary peace,' a senator thunders. But the strait is quieter than it has been in weeks.

choices.label: Counter-propose
choices.flavor: Negotiations drag on. Modest gains.
choices.consequence: You counter-propose: limited technology exchanges in non-strategic sectors, with quarterly reviews tied to Iranian behavior. The Chinese ambassador listens, nods, and relays the counter to Beijing. Negotiations drag on for two weeks, producing modest gains and a diplomatic relationship that is marginally warmer. The crisis continues, but a channel is open.

choices.label: Reject outright
choices.flavor: Hawks applaud. China is annoyed.
choices.consequence: You reject the proposal outright. 'China's technology ambitions and Iran's aggression are separate matters,' you tell the ambassador. Hawks in Congress applaud your firmness. The Chinese ambassador gathers his paper and leaves without a handshake. Within a month, Chinese arms shipments to Iran increase. The diplomatic window narrows.

events.scene: The Chinese ambassador requests a private meeting — no aides, no notes, no record. He arrives at the State Department through a side entrance, carrying nothing but a single sheet of paper. The proposal is simple: Beijing will pressure Iran to stand down in the strait in exchange for eased technology restrictions on three Chinese semiconductor firms. The ambassador slides the paper across the table. 'Minister Wang believes this crisis offers a unique opportunity for our nations to demonstrate cooperative leadership,' he says, his English impeccable. The offer is genuine. The price is steep. And the geopolitical implications extend far beyond the strait.

events.title: HOUTHI RED SEA ATTACK
events.description: Houthi forces have struck a commercial vessel in the Red Sea with an anti-ship missile. Iran's proxy war is expanding.

choices.label: Strike Houthi launch sites
choices.flavor: Precision strikes destroy launch infrastructure. Houthis go quiet briefly.
choices.consequence: Precision strikes light up the Yemeni coast. F/A-18s from the Eisenhower drop JDAMs on the launch site, the mobile radar unit, and two suspected ammunition storage facilities. The Houthi launch capability is degraded — for now. But the Houthis have survived worse and rebuilt faster, and the footage of explosions in Yemen will play very differently in the Arab world than in Washington.

choices.label: Ask Saudi Arabia to handle it
choices.flavor: Riyadh engages. Results are mixed but it shares the burden.
choices.consequence: You ask Saudi Arabia to handle it. The call to Riyadh is carefully framed: 'This is your neighborhood.' The Saudi military engages with a mix of airstrikes and ground-based interdiction. Results are mixed — the Houthis are experienced guerrilla fighters who learned to disperse after years of Saudi bombardment. But the burden is shared, and the optics of an Arab-led response are better than an American one.

choices.label: Condemn and do nothing
choices.flavor: Critics ask why you're not responding to attacks on global shipping.
choices.consequence: You condemn the attack in a formal statement and do nothing more. The press secretary reads the words with appropriate gravity. Critics immediately ask why the world's most powerful navy is watching commercial shipping get attacked. The question echoes through cable news for days, unanswered.

events.scene: The CENTCOM alert scrolls across every screen in the Situation Room: Houthi anti-ship missile launch detected from the Yemeni coast, tracking toward a Panamanian-flagged bulk carrier in the southern Red Sea. The missile is an Iranian-supplied C-802 derivative — you recognize it from the intelligence briefings. The carrier's crew of twenty-six has no idea what is heading their way at Mach 0.9. Your Red Sea desk officer is already on the phone with the Fifth Fleet. Iran's proxy war has expanded beyond the Gulf, and every day it remains unanswered, it grows bolder.

events.title: MILITIA BASE ATTACK
events.description: Iraqi militia rockets hit a US base in the region. Two soldiers wounded. CENTCOM wants authorization to retaliate.

choices.label: Retaliatory strike
choices.flavor: Precision strikes hit militia warehouses. A clear message sent.
choices.consequence: You authorize the retaliatory strike. Four hours after the attack, precision munitions destroy the militia's weapons warehouse and a command-and-control node. The message is clear and immediate: attack American forces, lose your infrastructure. In Baghdad, the militia's leadership goes to ground. The wounded soldier is medevacked to Landstuhl. The other soldier will carry shrapnel in his shoulder for the rest of his life.

choices.label: Increase base security
choices.flavor: You absorb the attack. Some call it restraint, others weakness.
choices.consequence: You order increased base security — additional patrols, counter-rocket systems, hardened shelters. You absorb the attack without retaliating. Some in the press call it restraint. Others call it weakness. The militia fires again six days later, from a different position. The soldiers on base begin sleeping in their body armor.

choices.label: Withdraw from the base
choices.flavor: The withdrawal is spun as a retreat. Iran claims victory.
choices.consequence: You order the withdrawal from the base. The logistics are completed in seventy-two hours — efficient, professional, and devastating to morale. Iranian state media runs the footage of empty hangars and lowered flags as a victory montage. 'The Americans flee,' the anchor says, smiling. Your troops watch the broadcast from their new position, sixty kilometers further from the fight.

events.scene: The rockets hit at 0317 local time — three 107mm Katyushas, launched from a pickup truck parked in a palm grove three kilometers from the base perimeter. Two impact inside the wire. Your base operations center calls it in: two soldiers wounded, one critically, shrapnel from a rocket that hit the chow hall. The video from the base security cameras shows the flash, the dust cloud, the soldiers running for cover in their underwear. CENTCOM's targeting cell has already identified the militia group — Kata'ib Hezbollah, Iranian-funded, Iranian-trained. The strike authorization request is on your desk. The blood on the chow hall floor is still wet.

events.title: DOMESTIC UNREST
events.description: Protests erupt in major cities over war spending and the strait crisis. The National Guard is requesting authorization.

choices.label: Address the nation
choices.flavor: Your speech calls for unity. Polls stabilize temporarily.
choices.consequence: You address the nation from the Oval Office. The speech is seventeen minutes long, and you write the final paragraph yourself. You speak of shared sacrifice, of the importance of the strait to every American family, of the path toward resolution. You do not promise it will be easy. The polls stabilize — not a surge, but the bleeding stops. The veteran in the wheelchair watches from his living room and nods, once.

choices.label: Deploy National Guard
choices.flavor: The crackdown makes headlines worldwide. Things get worse.
choices.consequence: You deploy the National Guard. The troops take up positions around federal buildings, their presence meant to deter violence. Instead, the images of uniformed soldiers facing American citizens make headlines around the world. 'Is this Tehran or DC?' a European newspaper asks. The protests grow larger. The crackdown has become the story.

choices.label: Announce peace initiative
choices.flavor: A bold move. Moderates applaud, hawks protest.
choices.consequence: You announce a peace initiative — a bold, public pivot toward diplomatic resolution. Moderates across the spectrum applaud. Peace organizations endorse it. But hawks in your own party and in the military establishment view it as capitulation under domestic pressure. The announcement satisfies neither the streets nor the war room.

events.scene: The aerial footage fills the Situation Room screens: protests in Washington, Chicago, Los Angeles, Houston. Tens of thousands in the streets, carrying signs that read 'NO WAR FOR OIL' and 'BRING THEM HOME.' A gas station in Detroit was set on fire overnight. The National Guard commander for the District of Columbia has submitted a formal request for authorization to deploy. Your domestic policy advisor sits across from you, her face drawn. 'The polling shows sixty-two percent oppose escalation,' she says. 'And thirty-eight percent believe the crisis is being mismanaged.' On the screen, a protester in a wheelchair — a veteran — is being interviewed by CNN. He is articulate, sympathetic, and devastating.

events.title: HOSTAGE SITUATION
events.description: Iran is holding 12 crew members from a seized tanker. Families are on every news channel.

choices.label: Negotiate release
choices.flavor: You open a channel through Swiss intermediaries. The crew is alive but scared.
choices.consequence: You open the channel through Bern. The Swiss ambassador in Tehran makes discreet contact. The negotiations begin slowly — Iran wants sanctions relief, you want proof of life. After seventy-two hours of back-and-forth, a video arrives: twelve crew members, alive, reading from prepared statements. The channel is open. The crew is scared but breathing.

choices.label: Demand unconditional release
choices.flavor: You increase pressure. International opinion turns against Iran.
choices.consequence: You demand unconditional release in a public statement, backed by a new round of targeted sanctions against IRGC Navy commanders. International opinion turns sharply against Iran — the UN Secretary General issues a personal statement calling for the crew's release. The pressure builds, but the crew remains in Iranian hands, and pressure does not equal progress.

choices.label: Offer quiet concession
choices.flavor: A minor sanctions waiver. The crew comes home. Nobody talks about the price.
choices.consequence: You authorize a quiet concession: a minor sanctions waiver on Iranian medical supplies, routed through an intermediary so the connection is deniable. Three days later, the crew is released at the Omani border, gaunt but alive. They fly home to tearful reunions broadcast worldwide. Nobody talks about the price. Nobody needs to.

events.scene: Twelve faces stare from the split screen in the Situation Room — passport photos of the crew seized from the tanker MV Coral Dawn, now held in an unknown location in Iran. A Filipino engineer. A Greek captain. An Indian second officer. Their families have been on CNN for three straight days. A mother from Mumbai breaks down on camera, her words translated in real time. Your hostage negotiation team has identified a potential channel through Swiss intermediaries, but Tehran is not responding. The IRGC is using the crew as leverage, and every hour they remain in captivity, the pressure on you intensifies.

events.title: GULF STATE OFFER
events.description: Saudi Arabia and UAE offer to fund 60% of your operations — but they want a say in rules of engagement.

choices.label: Accept with conditions
choices.flavor: Gulf funding flows in. You retain operational control.
choices.consequence: You accept with conditions — their money, your operational control. A carefully drafted memorandum of understanding gives Gulf partners consultation rights but preserves American command authority over all kinetic decisions. The money flows. Your budget stabilizes. Gulf partners feel invested, literally, in the outcome. The arrangement works because both sides need it to.

choices.label: Accept fully
choices.flavor: Generous funding helps but critics question who's calling the shots.
choices.consequence: You accept fully. Gulf funding pours in, generous and immediate. Your budget crisis evaporates overnight. But in the first week, the Saudi military liaison asks to review a strike package before authorization, and you realize that 'consultation' has different meanings in Riyadh than in Washington. Critics ask who is really calling the shots in the strait.

choices.label: Decline
choices.flavor: You maintain independence but miss burden-sharing.
choices.consequence: You decline. The Saudi ambassador absorbs the rejection with the practiced grace of a man accustomed to hearing 'no' from Americans. You maintain complete independence. Your budget continues to bleed. The opportunity for burden-sharing passes, and with it a chance to deepen Gulf partnerships that might outlast this crisis.

events.scene: The offer arrives through the Saudi ambassador, delivered over coffee at a private meeting that appears on no official schedule. Saudi Arabia and the UAE will fund sixty percent of your strait operations — patrol costs, intelligence sharing, logistics support. The number is staggering: hundreds of millions of dollars flowing from Gulf sovereign wealth funds to offset your hemorrhaging budget. The catch arrives in the second paragraph: they want a seat at the rules-of-engagement table. Not a veto, exactly. But 'meaningful consultation on kinetic decisions.' Your budget officer, who has been running deficit projections that keep him up at night, looks at you with something approaching hope.

events.title: DRONE SHOOT-DOWN
events.description: Iran shot down your surveillance drone over international waters. Joint Chiefs want a proportional response.

choices.label: Strike the missile battery
choices.flavor: Precision strike destroys the SAM site. The world holds its breath.
choices.consequence: You authorize the strike. A single Tomahawk destroys the SAM site with clinical precision. The explosion is captured by another drone — you always have another drone — and the footage is released to confirm the proportionality. The world holds its breath. Iran issues a furious condemnation but does not retaliate. The message has been sent, received, and understood.

choices.label: Respond with more drones
choices.flavor: You flood the area with drones. Iran doesn't dare shoot again.
choices.consequence: You flood the zone. Within twenty-four hours, the number of surveillance drones over the Gulf doubles. Where there was one MQ-9, now there are four. Iran watches them circle overhead and does not fire again. The implicit message is elegant: shoot one down, and we send more. The drone swarm becomes its own deterrent.

choices.label: Stand down — it was unmanned
choices.flavor: You absorb the loss. Some call it wise, others call it weak.
choices.consequence: You stand down. 'It was unmanned,' you tell the Joint Chiefs. 'No American blood was spilled. We absorb this.' The Chairman nods, his expression unreadable. Some in the press call it wisdom — proportional responses invite proportional counter-responses, and nobody dies over a drone. Others call it weakness. In Tehran, the calculus shifts: if we can shoot down their drones without consequence, what else can we do?

events.scene: The surveillance feed from the MQ-9 Reaper cuts to static at 0623 local time. Your drone, operating over international waters in the Gulf of Oman, has been destroyed by an Iranian surface-to-air missile. The telemetry data, recovered before the link was severed, shows the SA-15 battery that fired. The Joint Chiefs are in your office within the hour, the targeting package already prepared. 'Proportional response, sir,' the Chairman says, laying photos of the Iranian missile battery on your desk. 'We take out the battery that shot down our drone. Clean, professional, defensible.' The wreckage of your forty-million-dollar aircraft is sinking to the bottom of the Gulf of Oman.

events.title: UN RESOLUTION VOTE
events.description: A resolution condemning Iran is up for vote. Russia will veto unless you water it down.

choices.label: Push strong resolution
choices.flavor: Russia vetoes. But the debate isolates Iran diplomatically.
choices.consequence: You push the strong resolution. Russia vetoes, as promised — the ambassador's hand raised with theatrical regret. China follows. The resolution fails. But the debate itself — broadcast live to the world — has isolated Iran diplomatically. The transcript of the Iranian ambassador's defensive, stammering response plays on loop. Sometimes losing the vote wins the argument.

choices.label: Accept watered-down version
choices.flavor: Passes unanimously. Symbolic but shows unity.
choices.consequence: You accept the watered-down version. 'Deplores' instead of 'condemns.' 'Calls upon' instead of 'demands.' It passes unanimously — fifteen hands raised in a gesture of international consensus. The resolution is symbolic, toothless, and unanimous. Your team spins it as unity. The Iranian ambassador shrugs.

choices.label: Withdraw the resolution
choices.flavor: Allies are confused by the retreat.
choices.consequence: You withdraw the resolution entirely. Your ambassador explains that 'we seek a stronger framework than the current political landscape permits.' Allies are confused by the retreat. Russia is surprised. Iran is suspicious. The tactical withdrawal leaves everyone uncertain about your next move, which may be the point — or may be nothing at all.

events.scene: The Security Council chamber in New York is quiet with the particular tension of a vote that matters. The draft resolution condemning Iran's actions in the strait is on the table. Your ambassador has spent three days negotiating language — 'condemns' versus 'deplores,' 'demands' versus 'calls upon.' Russia's ambassador has made it clear, through intermediaries, that a veto awaits any resolution with enforcement teeth. China is performing its customary ambiguity, signaling different things to different delegations. The vote is sixty seconds away.

events.title: ELECTION YEAR PRESSURE
events.description: Your party is down in polls. Advisors want a dramatic move before midterms.

choices.label: Stage a naval exercise
choices.flavor: Carriers look great on camera. Polls bump. Iran calls it provocation.
choices.consequence: You stage a naval exercise — Operation Iron Shield. Two carrier groups, forty ships, visible from space. The footage is spectacular: jets launching, missiles firing, sailors standing at attention on flight decks. Polls bump three points overnight. Iran calls it 'dangerous provocation.' Your campaign team calls it 'presidential leadership.' The truth, as always, lives somewhere in between.

choices.label: Focus on diplomacy
choices.flavor: Not flashy. Pundits debate whether it's leadership or weakness.
choices.consequence: You focus on diplomacy. A quiet round of talks, a measured statement about de-escalation. The pundits debate whether it is leadership or weakness. Your poll numbers do not move. Your advisors are frustrated. But the diplomatic track advances by one small, invisible step that will not appear in any headline or any poll.

choices.label: Ignore the advisors
choices.flavor: You stay the course. The polls are what they are.
choices.consequence: You ignore the advisors. 'The crisis does not care about the election,' you tell them. 'And the election does not care about the crisis.' The polls are what they are. You govern based on the intelligence, the military assessment, and the diplomatic landscape. Your political team walks out deflated. History may vindicate you. The electorate may not.

events.scene: Your political advisors have taken over the Roosevelt Room, their laptops open to polling data that tells a grim story. 'Down six points in the suburbs,' your campaign manager says. 'Down nine among independents.' A dramatic move before the midterms could reverse the trend — or accelerate it. The television in the corner shows a cable news panel debating whether the strait crisis helps or hurts you politically. Your pollster slides a memo across the table: 'Voters respond to strength, not nuance.' The advisors want action. The question is whether you govern for the polls or govern for the policy.

events.title: PIPELINE SABOTAGE
events.description: An underwater pipeline feeding a major terminal has been damaged. Could be Iran, could be an accident.

choices.label: Blame Iran, escalate
choices.flavor: The accusation rallies allies but stokes the fire.
choices.consequence: You blame Iran publicly. The accusation is delivered from the State Department podium, backed by circumstantial evidence and intelligence assessments. Allies rally. Public opinion shifts. But in the intelligence community, analysts who are not yet certain exchange uncomfortable glances. If the forensics tell a different story, the accusation will become your credibility's grave.

choices.label: Investigate first
choices.flavor: Measured response. The investigation takes time.
choices.consequence: You investigate first. A methodical, public, transparent investigation — forensic divers, international observers, a timeline for findings. The markets hate the uncertainty. Cable news fills the vacuum with speculation. But the investigation will eventually produce facts, and facts are the one currency that does not depreciate.

choices.label: Offer joint investigation
choices.flavor: Surprisingly, Iran agrees. A thread of trust forms.
choices.consequence: You propose a joint investigation with Iran. The offer is unprecedented — a gesture of either radical trust or radical strategy. To everyone's surprise, Iran agrees. A team of American and Iranian engineers descends to the pipeline together. In the cold water, far from politics, professionals examine the evidence. A thread of trust forms in the most unlikely of places.

events.scene: The underwater pipeline feeding the Fujairah terminal has ruptured. Video from an ROV shows a clean cut in the steel — too precise for corrosion, too symmetrical for an anchor strike. Your intelligence analysts are divided: the Iranian intelligence service has the capability, but so do half a dozen non-state actors. The oil terminal's throughput has dropped forty percent. Your forensics team needs seventy-two hours to analyze the cut pattern, the explosive residue, and the underwater current data. In the meantime, oil markets have already rendered their verdict: futures up eight dollars on the word 'sabotage.'

events.title: RUSSIAN ARMS SHIPMENT
events.description: Intel shows Russia is shipping advanced anti-ship missiles to Iran. If they're deployed, the strait becomes deadlier.

choices.label: Intercept the shipment
choices.flavor: The ship is turned back. Russia is furious. Iran doesn't get the weapons.
choices.consequence: You intercept the shipment. A US Navy vessel hails the Akademik Chernov in international waters and orders it to heave to for inspection. Russia is furious — their ambassador calls it 'state piracy.' But the missiles do not reach Iran, and the diplomatic fallout, while severe, is manageable. Moscow files a protest at the UN. Tehran gets nothing.

choices.label: Diplomatic protest
choices.flavor: A formal protest. The shipment arrives but world opinion shifts.
choices.consequence: You issue a formal diplomatic protest. A strongly worded demarche is delivered to the Russian Foreign Ministry. The protest is noted, filed, and the shipment arrives on schedule. World opinion shifts slightly — Russia's role as arms supplier is now documented. But the missiles are deployed on the Iranian coast within two weeks.

choices.label: Ignore it
choices.flavor: The missiles are deployed. Iranian capabilities increase.
choices.consequence: You do nothing. The missiles are unloaded at Bandar-e Anzali, transported to coastal batteries, and operational within a month. Iranian anti-ship capability increases measurably. Your Navy adjusts its patrol patterns to account for the new threat geometry. The strait has become more dangerous, and the cost of inaction compounds daily.

events.scene: The satellite imagery is unambiguous: a Russian cargo vessel, the MV Akademik Chernov, transiting the Caspian Sea toward the Iranian port of Bandar-e Anzali. Her deck cargo, partially covered by tarps, shows the distinctive silhouette of Kh-35 anti-ship missile launchers. Your intelligence assessment estimates forty missiles in the shipment — enough to make the Strait of Hormuz significantly deadlier. The Russian ambassador has not returned your calls. The shipment will arrive in Iranian hands within seventy-two hours unless you act.

events.title: IRANIAN POWER STRUGGLE
events.description: Intel suggests moderates within Iran's government are challenging IRGC hardliners. You could tip the balance.

choices.label: Covert support for moderates
choices.flavor: Quiet support shifts the internal debate. IRGC loses influence.
choices.consequence: You authorize covert support — quiet channels, deniable funding, strategic intelligence sharing through third parties. The support shifts the internal debate incrementally. Araghchi's faction gains ground in the Supreme National Security Council. IRGC funding requests are questioned for the first time. The change is invisible from the outside, but inside Tehran's power structure, the plates are shifting.

choices.label: Public endorsement
choices.flavor: Your endorsement backfires — moderates are tainted by US association.
choices.consequence: You publicly endorse the moderate faction. Your speech, carried on BBC Persian, is eloquent and sincere. But in Tehran, the effect is catastrophic. The moderates are immediately labeled American puppets. IRGC propaganda organs run your speech alongside footage of past American interventions. Araghchi's position becomes untenable within days.

choices.label: Stay out of it
choices.flavor: You let Iranian politics play out on their own.
choices.consequence: You stay out of it entirely. Iranian politics will play out on Iranian terms, in Iranian time. Your analysts watch and report, but you issue no orders. The internal struggle continues without external interference. The outcome is uncertain, but whatever emerges will be genuinely Iranian — and therefore more durable than anything you could engineer.

events.scene: The CIA station chief in the region delivers the briefing personally, a rare signal of its importance. 'The moderate faction within Iran's government is challenging the IRGC hardliners,' she says, spreading intercepted communications across the table. 'Foreign Minister Araghchi has been making private arguments for de-escalation. He's gaining traction with the civilian government. But Tangsiri and the IRGC leadership are pushing back hard.' She looks at you directly. 'You have a narrow window to tip the balance. But any overt move could backfire — the moderates are already accused of being Western stooges.'

events.title: ESCORT CONVOY REQUEST
events.description: Multiple shipping companies request military escort through the strait. It would restore confidence but stretch your forces.

choices.label: Full escort operations
choices.flavor: Convoys resume under military escort. Oil markets stabilize.
choices.consequence: You launch full escort operations. Convoys form up in the Gulf of Oman and transit the strait under the watchful presence of American warships, their radars sweeping, their weapons armed. Oil markets stabilize within days. The shipping companies resume transit. It works — at the cost of operational flexibility and crew fatigue that will compound over weeks.

choices.label: Limited escort for allies only
choices.flavor: Allied flagged ships get protection. Others are on their own.
choices.consequence: You offer limited escort for allied-flagged vessels only. American, British, Japanese, and Australian tankers sail under protection. Ships flying flags of convenience — Liberia, Panama, Marshall Islands — are on their own. The policy is defensible but creates a two-tier system. Some tankers transit safely. Others reroute. The strait becomes a divided highway.

choices.label: Decline — too risky
choices.flavor: Shipping companies reroute. Costs skyrocket.
choices.consequence: You decline the request. The military assessment is clear: escort operations in a contested strait are an invitation to escalation. The shipping companies reroute around the Cape of Good Hope. Transit times double. Costs triple. Gas prices surge. Your inbox fills with letters from Congressional offices, each one angrier than the last.

events.scene: The request comes not from one shipping company but from twelve — a joint letter signed by Maersk, Evergreen, COSCO, and nine others, delivered to the State Department and the Pentagon simultaneously. They want military escort through the strait. Without it, they say, commercial transit will cease within two weeks. The insurance companies are already pulling coverage. Your naval commander in the Gulf has run the numbers: full escort operations would require every available destroyer, plus carrier air cover, plus mine countermeasures vessels. It is possible, but barely, and it would stretch your forces to the breaking point.

events.title: TANKER ESCAPE — USS MCFAUL
events.description: Six IRGC gunboats are converging on the tanker Stena Imperative. USS McFaul (DDG-74) is 12 minutes away. The tanker captain is requesting emergency escort.

choices.label: Full speed intercept — weapons hot
choices.flavor: McFaul arrives at flank speed. F-35 overhead shoots down an Iranian Shahed drone. IRGC boats scatter. The tanker escapes.
choices.consequence: You give the order: full speed intercept, weapons hot. The McFaul's turbines scream as she comes to flank speed, her bow cutting a white scar across the dark water. Overhead, the F-35 drops through the clouds and immediately engages an Iranian Shahed drone on a collision course with the tanker. The explosion is visible for miles. IRGC boats scatter as the destroyer's fire-control radar paints them. The Stena Imperative escapes. The tanker captain weeps with relief on the radio.

choices.label: Intercept but weapons tight
choices.flavor: McFaul positions between the tanker and gunboats. Tense standoff. IRGC withdraws after 40 minutes.
choices.consequence: You order McFaul to intercept but keep weapons tight. The destroyer positions herself between the tanker and the gunboats, her hull a steel wall. For forty minutes, the standoff holds — American sailors on deck, fingers near triggers, watching Iranian boats circle at two hundred meters. Finally, one by one, the IRGC boats peel away. The Stena Imperative sails free. No shots fired. No one sleeps well tonight.

choices.label: Monitor only — avoid provocation
choices.flavor: IRGC boards and seizes the Stena Imperative. Video goes viral. "Where was the Navy?"
choices.consequence: You order monitoring only. The tactical feed shows what happens next in high-definition: IRGC commandos board the Stena Imperative, replacing the Swedish flag with Iranian colors. The crew is ordered below. The video — captured by surveillance drones you ordered not to act — goes viral within the hour. 'Where was the Navy?' trends globally. The answer sits heavy in the room.

events.scene: The tactical display lights up with six hostile contacts converging on a single green icon. The Stena Imperative, a Swedish-flagged tanker, is under siege. IRGC gunboats — Heydar-class, the fastest combat boats on earth — are closing from three directions at fifty knots. The tanker's captain is on the distress frequency, his voice cracking with fear: 'Any warship, any warship, this is Stena Imperative, we are being approached by Iranian military vessels, request immediate assistance.' USS McFaul, DDG-74, is twelve minutes away at flank speed. An F-35 from the Eisenhower is eight minutes out. The clock is running, and the IRGC boats are not slowing down.

events.title: MARITIME INSURANCE COLLAPSE
events.description: Lloyd's of London and major maritime insurers have cancelled all war risk coverage in the Persian Gulf. No insurance = no tankers transit. Oil markets are panicking.

choices.label: Government-backed insurance program ($20B)
choices.flavor: A $20B government guarantee backstops the market. Tankers resume transit. Chubb signs as lead underwriter.
choices.consequence: You authorize a twenty-billion-dollar government-backed insurance program. The announcement is made jointly with the Treasury Secretary and the Secretary of Transportation. Chubb signs as lead underwriter within the day, backstopped by the full faith and credit of the United States. Tankers begin scheduling transit within forty-eight hours. The money is staggering, but the alternative — a closed strait — is worse.

choices.label: Pressure insurers to resume coverage
choices.flavor: Arm-twisting produces limited coverage at 300% premiums. Some tankers resume.
choices.consequence: You pressure the insurers to resume coverage. Phone calls from the Treasury Secretary, meetings with Lloyd's leadership, implied threats about regulatory scrutiny. The arm-twisting produces limited results: coverage resumes at three hundred percent normal premiums. Some tankers resume transit. Others decide the math does not work. The strait is half-open, which may be worse than fully closed.

choices.label: Let the market sort itself out
choices.flavor: Transit drops 70%. 150+ ships anchored outside the strait. Gas hits $6/gallon.
choices.consequence: You let the market sort itself out. No intervention, no backstop, no pressure. Transit drops seventy percent within a week. Over 150 ships sit at anchor outside the strait, their crews idle, their cargo undelivered. Gas hits six dollars a gallon in the American heartland. The free market has spoken, and its verdict is that the Strait of Hormuz is not worth the risk.

events.scene: The call from Lloyd's of London comes to your Treasury Secretary at 6 AM: all war risk coverage in the Persian Gulf has been cancelled, effective immediately. The ripple effect is instant and devastating. Without insurance, no tanker can legally transit. Without tankers, no oil moves. Without oil, the global economy begins to seize. Within hours, the market impact is visible: futures spike, shipping companies announce 'indefinite suspension' of Gulf operations, and your inbox fills with urgent messages from every energy-importing nation on earth. The maritime insurance market — a system older than America itself — has just declared the Strait of Hormuz uninsurable.

events.title: NUCLEAR BREAKOUT WARNING
events.description: IAEA reports Iran has moved enriched uranium to the underground Fordow facility. At 60% enrichment with 440kg stockpile, weapons-grade material is 72 hours away.

choices.label: Strike Fordow — bunker busters
choices.flavor: B-2 bombers drop GBU-57 MOPs on the mountain facility. Iran vows "devastating retaliation." The nuclear clock resets.
choices.consequence: You give the order. The B-2 Spirit lifts off from Whiteman Air Force Base carrying thirty-thousand-pound bunker busters. Twelve hours later, the mountain above Fordow collapses inward. The world watches in stunned silence. You have just committed an act of war against a sovereign nation's nuclear program. The consequences will unfold for decades.

choices.label: Emergency UN session — demand inspections
choices.flavor: The Security Council convenes. Russia and China abstain rather than veto. New inspections demanded.
choices.consequence: You pick up the phone and call the IAEA Director General. Within hours, a team of inspectors is en route to Tehran, armed with your intelligence package and a Security Council mandate. It is a gamble — trusting verification over force. The inspectors will either prove the threat or become hostages to it.

choices.label: Offer down-blend deal
choices.flavor: You offer sanctions relief in exchange for down-blending to 3.67%. Hawks call it appeasement. Iran considers it.
choices.consequence: You propose a deal. Direct talks, sanctions relief in exchange for dismantlement, a path that your own party will call surrender. The diplomatic cable goes out through back channels. You are betting that Iran wants survival more than it wants the bomb. History will judge whether you were right.

events.scene: The CIA Director's face is gray as old concrete when she walks into the Situation Room. She lays a single satellite photograph on the table — Fordow enrichment facility, buried deep in a mountain near Qom. The centrifuge cascades are spinning at ninety percent enrichment. 'They're weeks from a weapon, possibly days,' she says. The room falls silent. Every option on the table leads somewhere terrible.

events.title: SAUDI ARAMCO ATTACK
events.description: Drone swarm strikes Abqaiq-Khurais oil processing facility. 5.7 million barrels/day knocked offline — half of Saudi production. Oil futures spike 15% overnight.

choices.label: Retaliatory strikes on Iranian launch sites
choices.flavor: Cruise missiles hit IRGC drone bases. Iran denies involvement. The region braces for war.
choices.consequence: You authorize retaliatory strikes against the launch sites. Tomahawks arc across the Persian Gulf, hitting IRGC positions along the Iranian coast. The strikes are precise, devastating, and immediately escalatory. The price of oil jumps another twenty dollars a barrel before markets even open.

choices.label: Coordinate with Saudi air defense
choices.flavor: You deploy Patriot batteries and THAAD. Future attacks are intercepted. Production resumes in 10 days.
choices.consequence: You work the phones, calling Riyadh, London, and the UN Secretary General in rapid succession. A coalition response, measured and unified, begins to take shape. The Saudis want blood. You offer them something more useful — a plan.

choices.label: Condemn and investigate
choices.flavor: The investigation drags on. Oil prices stay elevated. Critics ask "What are you waiting for?"
choices.consequence: You hold your fire. The intelligence is murky, the attribution uncertain enough to give you cover. Markets panic, pundits howl, but you refuse to be stampeded into a wider war by a single attack.

events.scene: The attack comes at dawn, a swarm of cruise missiles and drones converging on Saudi Arabia's Abqaiq processing facility from multiple vectors. The facility handles five percent of global oil supply. Satellite imagery shows plumes of black smoke rising ten thousand feet into the Arabian sky. Saudi air defenses engaged some of the inbound weapons, but enough got through. Oil futures are already spiking.

events.title: HOUTHI RED SEA CAMPAIGN RESUMES
events.description: After months of quiet, Houthi forces launch anti-ship missiles at three commercial vessels in the Red Sea. Container shipping drops 90% through Bab el-Mandeb.

choices.label: Deploy carrier group to Red Sea
choices.flavor: Air strikes degrade Houthi launch sites. But now your forces are split between two theaters.
choices.consequence: You order strikes on Houthi launch sites and weapons depots in Yemen. The campaign begins within hours — cruise missiles slamming into mountain hideouts and coastal positions. The attacks degrade Houthi capability but scatter their fighters into the civilian population.

choices.label: Ask Saudi Arabia to handle it
choices.flavor: Riyadh engages but results are mixed. The Houthis are experienced guerrilla fighters.
choices.consequence: You coordinate with regional allies to establish a multinational naval escort through the Red Sea. Additional destroyers are repositioned, and intelligence sharing with Saudi and Emirati forces increases. It costs money and stretches your fleet thinner, but shipping lanes reopen.

choices.label: Focus on the strait — ignore Red Sea
choices.flavor: Shipping reroutes via Cape of Good Hope. 10 extra days, $1M fuel per voyage. Global supply chains buckle.
choices.consequence: You issue a diplomatic warning through Omani intermediaries, threatening consequences if attacks continue. The Houthis respond with a propaganda video showing their missile launchers. The attacks do not stop.

events.scene: CENTCOM's morning brief hits your desk with a red border. Houthi forces in Yemen have resumed attacks on commercial shipping in the Red Sea, launching anti-ship ballistic missiles at two tankers transiting the Bab el-Mandeb strait. One missile was intercepted by a Navy destroyer. The other struck a Panamanian-flagged chemical tanker, which is now listing and leaking cargo into the sea. The Houthis are Iran's most unpredictable proxy, and they smell opportunity in the Hormuz crisis.

events.title: IRAN BEGINS MINING THE STRAIT
events.description: CNN confirms: Iranian vessels are actively laying mines in the Strait of Hormuz shipping channels. CENTCOM identifies 12+ minelayers operating under cover of darkness.

choices.label: Sink the minelayers
choices.flavor: US forces sink 16 Iranian minelayers in a single night. Iran retains 80% of its small boat fleet.
choices.consequence: You authorize an immediate mine-clearing operation backed by naval air support. Helicopters sweep the shipping lanes while minesweepers inch forward through contested waters. It is dangerous, painstaking work, and every hour the lanes remain closed costs the global economy millions.

choices.label: Deploy minesweepers — clear lanes
choices.flavor: Allied minesweepers begin clearing operations. Transit resumes under escort within 72 hours.
choices.consequence: You assemble an international minesweeping coalition, calling in British, French, and Japanese assets to share the burden. The multinational approach is slower but spreads the risk and the political exposure across multiple governments.

choices.label: Establish exclusion zone
choices.flavor: Any vessel in the exclusion zone will be engaged. Iran calls it an act of war.
choices.consequence: You declare a maritime exclusion zone and announce that any vessel detected laying mines will be destroyed on sight. The declaration is a line drawn in salt water. Whether Iran crosses it will define the next phase of this crisis.

events.scene: A P-8 Poseidon maritime patrol aircraft spots them first — small boats operating under cover of darkness in the narrowest section of the Strait. Infrared cameras capture the unmistakable silhouette of naval mines being rolled off the sterns. By morning, your minesweeper crews confirm it: Iran is mining the Strait of Hormuz. Every tanker captain in the Gulf is now recalculating risk. Insurance rates triple before lunch.

events.title: ASIAN ENERGY EMERGENCY
events.description: India, Japan, and South Korea declare energy emergencies. 89% of strait oil goes to Asia. India invokes emergency powers to redirect LPG from industry to households. Tokyo warns of rolling blackouts.

choices.label: Coordinate allied reserve release
