choices.flavor: IEA coordinates the largest coordinated reserve release in history. Markets stabilize briefly.
choices.consequence: You authorize emergency oil shipments from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve and coordinate releases with allied nations. Tankers laden with American crude set course for Asian ports. It is expensive and depletes your reserves, but it buys goodwill and time.

choices.label: Prioritize US allies
choices.flavor: Allied tankers get priority escort. India and others are left scrambling.
choices.consequence: You tell the Asian allies to increase pressure on Iran directly, leveraging their own economic relationships with Tehran. The approach spreads the burden but risks fracturing the coalition if each nation cuts its own deal.

choices.label: Use as leverage for coalition
choices.flavor: "You want oil? Send warships." Japan and South Korea reluctantly agree to contribute naval assets.
choices.consequence: You propose an emergency energy summit, bringing affected nations together to coordinate a unified response. The summit builds solidarity and produces a joint statement, though critics note that statements do not power factories.

events.scene: The cascading effects of the Strait closure have reached Asia. Japan, South Korea, and India — nations that depend on Gulf oil for the majority of their energy imports — are facing rolling blackouts and industrial shutdowns. Tokyo's ambassador delivers a blunt message: 'Resolve this, or we will find our own solution.' The global economy is buckling, and the pressure to act is coming from every direction.

events.title: WAR POWERS RESOLUTION
events.description: Both chambers of Congress have drafted War Powers Resolutions. If passed, your military options are severely constrained. The vote is in 48 hours.

choices.label: Lobby to defeat it
choices.flavor: Republicans rally. The resolution fails narrowly in the Senate. Democrats vow to try again.
choices.consequence: You lobby hard against the resolution, spending political capital to maintain executive authority. Phone calls, favors, threats — the machinery of presidential persuasion runs at full power. You win the vote, but the margin is razor-thin and the resentment lingers.

choices.label: Declassify intel to justify actions
choices.flavor: Senators see the classified briefing. The resolution is tabled. But Iran now knows what you know.
choices.consequence: You work with congressional leadership to craft a compromise resolution that preserves your flexibility while giving Congress a voice. The bipartisan approach costs you some freedom of action but strengthens your domestic mandate.

choices.label: Accept constraints — show respect for process
choices.flavor: Critics call it weakness. Allies call it maturity. Your military options narrow but legitimacy grows.
choices.consequence: You publicly welcome the congressional debate, framing it as democracy in action during a time of crisis. The gesture earns praise from the press and builds a broader coalition of support, even if the final resolution constrains your options.

events.scene: The Speaker of the House has scheduled a War Powers Resolution vote for tomorrow. The debate on the floor is already bitter — hawks accusing doves of weakness, doves accusing hawks of warmongering. Your legislative team counts votes through the night. The outcome will either authorize expanded military operations or force a drawdown that could embolden Iran. Either way, the decision is no longer yours alone.

events.title: OPERATION EARNEST WILL 2.0
events.description: Pentagon proposes a formal convoy escort operation through the strait — echoing the 1987-88 Tanker War. Reflagging allied tankers under US flag for legal protection.

choices.label: Launch Earnest Will 2.0
choices.flavor: Reflagged tankers sail under the Stars and Stripes. Any attack is now an attack on America. Oil markets respond.
choices.consequence: You authorize a full convoy escort system, assigning warships to shepherd tankers through the Strait. The operation is massive — every available destroyer and frigate reassigned to escort duty. Oil begins to flow again, but your fleet is stretched to breaking and every transit is a potential flashpoint.

choices.label: Coalition-led escort (share the burden)
choices.flavor: UK, France, and Australia contribute escorts. Slower to organize but spreads the risk.
choices.consequence: You implement a partial escort program, prioritizing the most critical tanker routes while leaving secondary traffic to navigate independently. The half-measure satisfies no one completely but keeps your forces flexible.

choices.label: Too provocative — decline
choices.flavor: Shipping companies continue rerouting via Cape of Good Hope. Transit times and costs skyrocket.
choices.consequence: You reject the convoy model and focus on deterrence instead, positioning overwhelming naval force at the chokepoints without direct escort duties. The approach is less protective but avoids tying your ships to slow-moving tankers.

events.scene: The parallels are impossible to ignore. Your military historians have pulled the files from the 1987-88 Tanker War, and the similarities are haunting — Iranian attacks on commercial shipping, American convoy escorts, the constant threat of escalation. Admiral Hayes presents Operation Earnest Will as a template. 'It worked then,' he says. 'It can work now.' But you know how that story ended — with the USS Vincennes shooting down an Iranian airliner.

events.title: "MASSIVE ARMADA HEADING TO IRAN"
events.description: The President posts on Truth Social: "A massive Armada is heading to Iran. They will learn!" The post has 50 million views. Iran's Supreme Leader responds within the hour.

choices.label: Double down — back it with action
choices.flavor: You mobilize visibly. Iran calls it bluffing until the first carrier arrives. Then they go quiet.
choices.consequence: You double down, turning the rhetoric into reality by announcing a major naval buildup. If the world thinks you are sending an armada, then by God you will send one. The bluster becomes policy, and Iran watches a stream of warships enter the Gulf.

choices.label: Walk it back through spokespersons
choices.flavor: "The President was speaking metaphorically." Nobody buys it. Iran smells weakness.
choices.consequence: You walk it back, issuing a carefully worded statement that reframes the post as 'aspirational language about American strength.' The diplomatic community exhales. Your credibility takes a dent, but the immediate crisis passes.

choices.label: Use the chaos as cover for diplomacy
choices.flavor: While the world watches the tweet storm, back-channel talks advance quietly.
choices.consequence: You channel the attention into a diplomatic initiative, using the world's eyes as leverage to call for multilateral negotiations. The chaos becomes an opportunity, if you can steer it.

events.scene: You wake to find that a senior official has posted on social media about sending 'the greatest armada the world has ever seen' to the Persian Gulf. The post has gone viral. Allies are confused, adversaries are calculating, and your communications team is in damage-control mode. The markets have already reacted — defense stocks are surging, oil futures climbing. Your national security advisor is standing in your doorway, looking like someone who has swallowed a grenade.

events.title: IRANIAN MODERATES VS IRGC
events.description: Iran's civilian government and IRGC are openly clashing. The Foreign Ministry wants negotiations. The IRGC is planning more seizures. Your intel shows the split is real.

choices.label: Covert support for moderates
choices.flavor: Quiet channels funnel support. The moderates gain ground in internal debates. IRGC funding gets questioned.
choices.consequence: You reach out through back channels to the moderate faction, offering quiet assurances that de-escalation will be met with sanctions relief. The overture is risky — if the hardliners discover it, the moderates are finished. But if it works, you may end this without a shot.

choices.label: Tighten sanctions — squeeze both sides
choices.flavor: Maximum pressure doesn't discriminate. The moderates are weakened along with the IRGC.
choices.consequence: You tighten sanctions further, targeting the IRGC's business empire specifically, hoping to empower the moderates by weakening their rivals. The economic pressure is a scalpel, aimed at the regime's internal power dynamics.

choices.label: Public overture to moderates
choices.flavor: Your public endorsement backfires. Moderates are labeled American puppets. IRGC hardens.
choices.consequence: You maintain your current posture, unwilling to tip your hand while the internal struggle plays out. Sometimes the best move is to do nothing and let your adversary's contradictions do the work.

events.scene: Your intelligence analysts have identified a fracture within the Iranian regime. The IRGC hardliners under General Tangsiri are pushing for maximum confrontation, while the foreign ministry faction led by Araghchi advocates negotiation. Intercepted communications suggest the Supreme Leader has not yet chosen a side. This internal struggle is your opening — or your trap.

events.title: IRGC FAST BOAT SWARM
events.description: TWENTY-FIVE Heydar-class fast boats — the fastest combat boats on earth at 110 knots — are converging on a tanker convoy. This is Iran's signature tactic: overwhelm with speed and numbers.

choices.label: Weapons free — engage the swarm
choices.flavor: Phalanx CIWS and .50 cal fire. Eight boats destroyed. The rest scatter. First naval combat since 1988.
choices.consequence: You authorize weapons-free. The engagement is short and brutal — American firepower against Iranian numbers. Fast boats explode and capsize across miles of ocean. The swarm is broken, but the body count is staggering, and the footage will be on Al Jazeera within the hour.

choices.label: Warning shots and countermeasures
choices.flavor: Flares, warning shots, and acoustic devices. The swarm breaks off. For now.
choices.consequence: You authorize warning shots and aggressive maneuvering, hoping to disperse the swarm without a full engagement. Your destroyer captains play a deadly game of chicken at forty knots, their wakes cutting through the formation.

choices.label: Evasive maneuvers — protect the convoy
choices.flavor: The convoy scatters. One tanker is boarded. "Why didn't we shoot?" headlines dominate.
choices.consequence: You order your fleet to fall back and form a defensive perimeter around the tanker convoy, refusing to be drawn into a melee that could scatter your forces. The swarm probes your defenses, testing for weakness.

events.scene: CENTCOM reports a mass sortie of IRGC fast boats from multiple Iranian ports simultaneously. Dozens of armed speedboats are converging on the shipping lanes in a coordinated swarm pattern. Your fleet commanders are requesting weapons-free authorization. The radar screens look like a kicked anthill. This is the scenario your war planners have war-gamed a hundred times, and it is happening now.

events.title: HEZBOLLAH OPENS SECOND FRONT
events.description: Hezbollah launches missiles and drones into northern Israel from Lebanon. Iran's proxy war is expanding beyond the Gulf. Israel demands US support.

choices.label: Support Israel — provide intel and munitions
choices.flavor: US munitions flow to Israel. Hezbollah positions are degraded. But the US is now fighting on two fronts.
choices.consequence: You strike Hezbollah positions in Lebanon and Syria simultaneously, sending a message that proxy warfare has a price. The strikes are precise but the political fallout in Beirut is severe. The Lebanese government condemns the attacks.

choices.label: Call for ceasefire through UN
choices.flavor: The UN resolution passes. Hezbollah pauses. But everyone knows it's temporary.
choices.consequence: You work through diplomatic channels, pressuring Lebanon's government and engaging with European allies to isolate Hezbollah politically. The approach is slower but avoids widening the war to a third country.

choices.label: Stay out — this is Israel's fight
choices.flavor: Israel is disappointed but capable. The conflict expands without direct US involvement.
choices.consequence: You absorb the attacks, reinforcing your positions but refusing to be drawn into a multi-front conflict. The proxy strikes continue, but your strategic focus remains on the Strait.

events.scene: Hezbollah has opened a second front. Rockets are falling on the US Embassy compound in Baghdad, and your forces at Al-Tanf garrison in Syria are under sustained mortar fire. The proxy network is activating across the region, stretching your attention and your resources. The Iranian strategy is becoming clear — they will fight you everywhere except Iran.

events.title: CAPE OF GOOD HOPE BOTTLENECK
events.description: With the strait effectively closed, 150+ tankers are rerouting via the Cape of Good Hope — adding 11,000 nautical miles, 10 days, and $1M fuel per voyage. Ports in South Africa are overwhelmed.

choices.label: Subsidize rerouting costs
choices.flavor: Government subsidies keep tankers moving. It's expensive but it works.
choices.consequence: You subsidize the Cape route transits, offering insurance guarantees and fuel cost offsets to keep goods flowing. The price tag is enormous, but it keeps the global economy on life support while you work the strategic problem.

choices.label: Demand strait reopening as red line
choices.flavor: A formal ultimatum. Iran has 72 hours to guarantee safe passage. The clock ticks.
choices.consequence: You escalate naval operations in the Strait, arguing that the only solution to the shipping crisis is to reopen the direct route by force if necessary. The military option carries risks, but the economic alternative is already a disaster.

choices.label: Accept the new normal
choices.flavor: Markets price in the permanent disruption. The "new Suez" route becomes standard. Costs cascade through the global economy.
choices.consequence: You convene an emergency G7 summit to coordinate the economic response, pushing for burden-sharing and joint action on strategic petroleum reserves. The summit produces commitments, though implementation will lag behind promises.

events.scene: Shipping companies are rerouting around the Cape of Good Hope to avoid the Strait entirely. The alternative route adds two weeks and massive fuel costs to every tanker voyage. The global supply chain is warping under the pressure — delivery times extending, costs skyrocketing, industries that depend on just-in-time logistics grinding to a halt. Your economic advisors present models showing a global recession within sixty days if the Strait remains contested.

events.title: SUPREME LEADER SUCCESSION CRISIS
events.description: With Khamenei dead, his son Mojtaba is consolidating power with IRGC backing. But the Assembly of Experts is divided. Iran's internal chaos creates both danger and opportunity.

choices.label: Covert ops to empower moderates
choices.flavor: CIA channels support Raisi-faction moderates. The succession battle intensifies. IRGC is distracted.
choices.consequence: You instruct your intelligence services to monitor the succession closely, redirecting satellite coverage and SIGINT assets toward Tehran's power centers. Understanding who controls Iran tomorrow is worth more than any missile strike today.

choices.label: Publicly demand democratic transition
choices.flavor: Your call for democracy rallies Western opinion but unites Iranian factions against external meddling.
choices.consequence: You publicly comment on the leadership transition, framing it as an opportunity for a 'new chapter' in relations. The statement is calculated to unsettle the hardliners and encourage the moderates. Iran's state media erupts in fury.

choices.label: Exploit the chaos — escalate strikes
choices.flavor: You hit command centers while leadership is in disarray. Effective but brutal. The world recoils.
choices.consequence: You accelerate military pressure, calculating that a regime in transition is a regime vulnerable to coercion. The strikes intensify as Tehran's power brokers look inward, distracted by succession politics.

events.scene: Your intelligence briefer delivers the morning's bombshell quietly, almost casually. The Supreme Leader's health is failing, and his son Mojtaba Khamenei is positioning himself to inherit power. The IRGC generals are divided — some support Mojtaba, others see him as a weak pretender. Iran's internal politics are about to become the most dangerous variable in your crisis equation.

events.title: NSA KENT RESIGNS
events.description: National Security Advisor Joe Kent has resigned, citing disagreements over escalation. He goes on Fox News within the hour. "The President is being misled by hawks who've never seen combat."

choices.label: Attack Kent publicly
choices.flavor: A public feud dominates the news cycle. Your war cabinet looks fractured.
choices.consequence: You let the resignation stand and refuse to engage publicly. Your press secretary issues a terse statement thanking the official for their service. The news cycle churns, but you give it nothing to feed on. The story fades in days, though the damage to morale lingers.

choices.label: Replace quietly — project unity
choices.flavor: The new NSA takes over smoothly. But Kent's book deal is already signed.
choices.consequence: You respond with a measured statement acknowledging disagreements while emphasizing the complexity of the crisis. You promote a respected deputy to fill the role, signaling stability and continuity.

choices.label: Reach out — bring him back with concessions
choices.flavor: Kent returns with a mandate for restraint. Hawks are furious but the team is reunited.
choices.consequence: You address the nation directly, using the resignation as a catalyst for a broader strategic recalibration. You announce a policy review and invite congressional consultation, turning a crisis of confidence into a display of leadership.

events.scene: The resignation letter lands on your desk at 0600, leaked to the press by 0615. A senior national security official — a decorated combat veteran with deep credibility in the defense community — has quit, publicly condemning your handling of the Iran crisis. The letter is devastating, detailed, and already trending. Cable news has him booked for every primetime show tonight.

events.title: GCC BASES UNDER FIRE
events.description: Iran launches ballistic missiles at Al Udeid (Qatar) and Al Dhafra (UAE). Both house US forces. Patriot batteries intercept most but three impact inside Al Udeid. 8 US casualties.

choices.label: Massive retaliation — hit IRGC bases
choices.flavor: B-2s and Tomahawks devastate IRGC coastal bases. Iran's ability to project force is degraded. But the war deepens.
choices.consequence: You launch a massive retaliatory strike package against Iranian military targets — airbases, missile batteries, naval installations. The operation is the largest American military action since the Iraq War. The Gulf allies cheer. The rest of the world holds its breath.

choices.label: Proportional response — missile batteries only
choices.flavor: Precision strikes eliminate the launch sites. Measured but firm. Allies approve.
choices.consequence: You coordinate a joint GCC-American response, sharing targeting data and command structures with allied forces. The coalition strike is smaller but politically powerful, demonstrating unified resolve against Iranian aggression.

choices.label: Evacuate non-essential personnel
choices.flavor: You pull back from exposed positions. Critics call it retreat. Families of the fallen demand action.
choices.consequence: You urge restraint, arguing that a measured response will prevent further escalation. The Gulf monarchs are incredulous. Your phone calls become shorter, the tones colder. The alliance strains under the weight of unmet expectations.

events.scene: The attacks are simultaneous and coordinated. Ballistic missiles strike a Saudi military base, a drone swarm targets a UAE oil terminal, and rockets hit a Bahraini naval facility. The Gulf Cooperation Council states are screaming for an American response. Your allies' capitals are burning, and the pressure to act is unlike anything you have felt. The Gulf monarchs are on the phone, one after another, each more furious than the last.

events.title: IRIS DENA WRECKAGE
events.description: The Iranian frigate IRIS Dena was sunk on Day 1. Now bodies and wreckage are washing up on Omani beaches. Al Jazeera is broadcasting it live. International pressure mounts.

choices.label: Express regret — offer humanitarian assistance
choices.flavor: A rare gesture of humanity in wartime. Some call it weakness. Others call it leadership.
choices.consequence: You order the rescued sailors returned immediately through Red Cross channels, making the humanitarian gesture as public and clean as possible. The world sees American hands pulling Iranian sailors from the sea. Tehran's propaganda machine stutters.

choices.label: Blame Iran for putting sailors in harm's way
choices.flavor: The messaging works at home. Internationally, it falls flat.
choices.consequence: You hold the rescued sailors as bargaining chips, leveraging their return in exchange for concessions on detained tanker crews. The calculation is cold but pragmatic. International law experts on your staff exchange nervous glances.

choices.label: No comment — focus on operations
choices.flavor: The silence speaks volumes. The story dominates for 48 hours.
choices.consequence: You stage a media event around the rescue, inviting embedded journalists to document American sailors providing medical care to their Iranian counterparts. The images cut through the fog of war with unexpected clarity.

events.scene: The IRIS Dena, an Iranian frigate, lies crippled in the Gulf of Oman after an engagement with coalition naval forces. Rescue operations are underway — Iranian sailors in the water, some wounded, all of them pawns in a game played at much higher altitudes. The footage of American sailors pulling Iranian sailors from the oil-slicked water is simultaneously a humanitarian triumph and a propaganda minefield.

events.title: BEIJING CEASEFIRE PROPOSAL
events.description: Chinese FM Wang Yi proposes a 5-point ceasefire framework: mutual de-escalation, UN peacekeepers in strait, sanctions relief timeline, nuclear talks restart, regional security forum. Beijing is serious.

choices.label: Accept the framework as starting point
choices.flavor: Talks begin in Beijing. Hawks rage about "selling out to China." But the guns go quiet for the first time in weeks.
choices.consequence: You engage with the Chinese proposal in good faith, sending your own envoy to Beijing for trilateral discussions. The talks are grueling — three languages, three sets of interests, three definitions of victory. But for the first time, there is a framework.

choices.label: Counter-propose (US-led framework)
choices.flavor: You reject Chinese mediation but propose your own plan. Beijing is annoyed but the diplomatic track opens.
choices.consequence: You reject the Chinese framework but counter with your own proposal, maintaining American primacy in Gulf security while addressing some of Beijing's concerns. The diplomatic chess match intensifies.

choices.label: Reject — China is not a neutral broker
choices.flavor: Hawks applaud. China retaliates with arms sales to Iran. The diplomatic window narrows.
choices.consequence: You publicly dismiss Chinese mediation as a power grab, rallying allies around an American-led solution. Beijing's face is bruised, and the channel closes. Your domestic audience applauds, but a potential off-ramp has vanished.

events.scene: Beijing has made its move. China's foreign minister arrives in Tehran with a comprehensive peace proposal, brokered in quiet consultations with both sides. The plan calls for mutual de-escalation, sanctions relief tied to verifiable milestones, and Chinese peacekeepers in the Strait. It is elegant, ambitious, and designed to position Beijing as the indispensable power broker of the twenty-first century. Your allies are intrigued. Your hawks are appalled.

events.title: NOWRUZ CEASEFIRE WINDOW
events.description: Iranian New Year (Nowruz) is in 3 days. Iranian moderates propose a 5-day ceasefire for the holiday. IRGC hasn't objected. This could be the off-ramp.

choices.label: Accept — extend to 10 days
choices.flavor: Both sides stand down. Tankers transit. The ceasefire holds. Diplomats work feverishly to make it permanent.
choices.consequence: You accept the Nowruz ceasefire and order a full stand-down of offensive operations. The silence that falls over the Gulf is profound and fragile. Every hour that passes without violence becomes an argument for peace. You hold your breath and wait.

choices.label: Accept 5 days only
choices.flavor: A brief respite. Oil flows. Markets rally. But everyone knows the clock is ticking.
choices.consequence: You accept a limited ceasefire covering the Strait itself while maintaining defensive operations elsewhere. The half-measure satisfies neither doves nor hawks, but it reduces the immediate risk of catastrophe while preserving your options.

choices.label: Reject — no pause while tankers are held
choices.flavor: You demand tanker release as precondition. Iran calls you unreasonable. The fighting continues through Nowruz.
choices.consequence: You reject the ceasefire, arguing that Iran is using the holiday as cover for military repositioning. The strikes continue through Nowruz. Iran's moderates lose their last argument for diplomacy.

events.scene: The Omani intermediary arrives with an unexpected proposal: Iran is offering a ceasefire timed to Nowruz, the Persian New Year. The gesture carries deep cultural significance — a fresh start, a new beginning. Your Iran experts see it as a genuine olive branch from the moderate faction. Your military advisors see it as a ploy to reposition forces. Both could be right.

events.title: HEZBOLLAH BREAKS CEASEFIRE
events.description: Hezbollah launches 150 rockets into northern Israel, breaking the Lebanon ceasefire. Israel retaliates immediately. Iran's proxy network is activating across the region.

choices.label: Support Israel — joint strikes on Hezbollah
choices.flavor: US and Israeli jets hit Hezbollah positions. The proxy front collapses. But you're now fighting a multi-front war.
choices.consequence: You strike back hard, targeting Hezbollah weapons depots and command nodes across Lebanon and Syria. The response is disproportionate by design — a message that ceasefire violations carry an exponential cost.

choices.label: Demand ceasefire restoration through UN
choices.flavor: French-led mediation produces a fragile truce. Hezbollah pulls back. For now.
choices.consequence: You respond through diplomatic channels, giving Iran forty-eight hours to rein in its proxy or face consequences. The ultimatum is public and binding. Tehran's response will reveal whether the moderate faction has any real control.

choices.label: Focus on strait — Israel can handle it
choices.flavor: You keep your eyes on the prize. Israel is capable. But the multi-front pressure on Iran lessens.
choices.consequence: You absorb the attack and maintain the broader ceasefire framework, treating Hezbollah's violation as a spoiler action by hardliners trying to torpedo negotiations. The restraint is agonizing but calculated.

events.scene: The ceasefire is barely hours old when Hezbollah launches a barrage of rockets from southern Lebanon toward a US logistics base in northern Iraq. The attack kills no one — the base was evacuated as a precaution — but the violation is brazen. Tehran claims Hezbollah acts independently. Nobody in your Situation Room believes that for a second.

events.title: HOUTHIS TARGET US WARSHIP
events.description: Houthi anti-ship ballistic missile targets USS Gravely (DDG-107) in the Red Sea. SM-2 intercept at 8nm. The closest a Houthi missile has come to hitting a US warship.

choices.label: Massive strike on Houthi infrastructure
choices.flavor: B-2s flatten Houthi missile storage. The Red Sea calms. Yemen civilians pay the price.
choices.consequence: You launch a sustained air campaign against Houthi military infrastructure in Yemen, deploying B-1 bombers alongside carrier-based aircraft. The strikes are devastating but the humanitarian implications in war-torn Yemen are severe.

choices.label: Tighten the naval screen
choices.flavor: More AEGIS destroyers deployed. An expensive but defensive solution.
choices.consequence: You expand the naval escort program to cover both the Strait of Hormuz and the Red Sea approaches, stretching your fleet across two theaters. The logistical challenge is immense but the shipping lanes must stay open.

choices.label: Pressure Saudi to negotiate with Houthis
choices.flavor: Riyadh reopens the Yemen peace track. Slow but addresses root causes.
choices.consequence: You work through Saudi and Emirati intermediaries to negotiate a separate Houthi stand-down, offering indirect concessions on the Yemen conflict in exchange for a cessation of maritime attacks. The diplomacy is messy and morally ambiguous.

events.scene: Houthi forces in Yemen have escalated dramatically, launching their most sophisticated attack yet — a coordinated barrage of anti-ship ballistic missiles and explosive-laden drone boats targeting a convoy of tankers in the Red Sea. Your destroyers intercept most of the incoming, but one tanker takes a hit and is ablaze. The Bab el-Mandeb strait, the world's second-most critical oil chokepoint, is now as dangerous as Hormuz.

events.title: KC-135 CRASH — COMBAT LOSSES MOUNT
events.description: A KC-135 aerial refueling tanker crashes during combat operations, killing all 3 crew. It's the first US aircraft loss. Cable news shows the wreckage on loop.

choices.label: Honor the fallen — steel resolve
choices.flavor: A solemn ceremony at Dover. The nation mourns but rallies. "Their sacrifice will not be in vain."
choices.consequence: You go public immediately with the facts as you know them, honoring the crew and committing to a full investigation. The transparency earns trust even as it exposes uncertainty. The families deserve the truth, even an incomplete one.

choices.label: Review operations — reduce tempo
choices.flavor: Sortie rates drop 40%. Safety improves. Hawks worry about losing momentum.
choices.consequence: You order a pause in air operations pending investigation, pulling tanker aircraft from their refueling orbits until the cause is determined. The operational impact is significant — strike range shrinks, sortie rates drop — but you will not lose more crews to a preventable cause.

choices.label: Escalate — make their sacrifice count
choices.flavor: The loss fuels a surge. More targets struck. The cycle accelerates.
choices.consequence: You classify the incident and accelerate operations, refusing to let a single loss dictate the tempo of your campaign. The crews understand risk. The mission continues. Your critics will call it callous. Your commanders call it necessary.

events.scene: A KC-135 aerial refueling tanker has gone down in the Arabian Sea during a routine mission. Four crew members are missing. Search and rescue operations are underway, but the crash site is in contested waters. Iran's navy is in the area. The cause is unknown — mechanical failure, hostile action, or something in between. The families are being notified. The press is asking questions you cannot yet answer.

events.title: ICC WAR CRIMES INVESTIGATION
events.description: The International Criminal Court announces an investigation into both US and Iranian conduct. Warrants could be issued for senior officials. European allies are divided.

choices.label: Reject ICC jurisdiction — sanction the court
choices.flavor: America First crowd loves it. Europeans are horrified. The legal threat remains for allied officials.
choices.consequence: You reject the ICC's jurisdiction outright, citing longstanding American policy and threatening sanctions against the court. The defiance plays well domestically but isolates you from European allies who support the tribunal.

choices.label: Cooperate partially — show good faith
choices.flavor: You provide limited documentation. It buys goodwill. The investigation slows.
choices.consequence: You cooperate with the investigation, providing evidence that you believe will exonerate American forces while exposing Iranian violations. The approach is risky — you are submitting to a process you do not control — but it demonstrates commitment to the rule of law.

choices.label: Use it as leverage against Iran
choices.flavor: You frame it as mutual accountability. Iran's crimes are documented alongside yours. A diplomatic tool emerges.
choices.consequence: You engage with the ICC selectively, cooperating on specific inquiries while challenging the court's broader mandate. The middle path satisfies no one perfectly but avoids the extremes of defiance and submission.

events.scene: The International Criminal Court has announced a preliminary investigation into potential war crimes committed during the Strait crisis. The investigation targets both sides, but American actions — the airstrikes, the blockade enforcement, the naval engagements — are under particular scrutiny. Your legal team is divided on the exposure. The political implications are enormous.

events.title: MOJTABA KHAMENEI POWER GRAB
events.description: Intelligence confirms Mojtaba Khamenei — the dead Supreme Leader's son — has consolidated IRGC support. He's demanding revenge strikes and threatening to close the strait permanently.

choices.label: Offer to recognize new leadership in exchange for de-escalation
choices.flavor: A bitter pill. Recognizing Khamenei's son feels like losing. But the back-channel opens.
choices.consequence: You extend a diplomatic overture to the new regime, proposing direct talks without preconditions. The offer is a test of Mojtaba's intentions and a signal that America is willing to deal with whoever holds power in Tehran.

choices.label: Target Mojtaba with a kill order
choices.flavor: Decapitation strike 2.0. If you miss, it's war. If you hit, there's nobody left to negotiate with.
choices.consequence: You increase pressure immediately, calculating that a new leader needs a demonstration of strength to consolidate internally and that showing weakness now will be exploited. Sanctions tighten, naval patrols intensify.

choices.label: Ignore him — focus on the military threat
choices.flavor: You treat the political question as irrelevant. The IRGC reads this as weakness.
choices.consequence: You wait and watch, committing additional intelligence resources to understanding Mojtaba's inner circle while maintaining your current posture. The patience is deliberate — you will not make the first move until you understand the board.

events.scene: Mojtaba Khamenei has consolidated power faster than anyone predicted. The IRGC generals who opposed him have been quietly retired or reassigned. Tehran's new power structure is taking shape, and it is unclear whether Mojtaba will prove more moderate or more extreme than his father. Your intelligence is contradictory — some sources say he wants a deal, others say he is preparing for war. The uncertainty is paralyzing.

events.title: AL UDEID BASE UNDER FIRE
events.description: Iranian ballistic missiles hit Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar — the forward HQ of CENTCOM. 3 US personnel killed, 22 wounded. Qatar is demanding you relocate operations.

choices.label: Retaliatory strike on launch site
choices.flavor: Tomahawks fly within 20 minutes. The base is avenged. But the escalation ladder climbs.
choices.consequence: You authorize an immediate retaliatory strike against the missile launch sites and the IRGC command center that ordered the attack. The response is swift, precise, and designed to demonstrate that attacking American soil — even rented soil — carries an unacceptable price.

choices.label: Relocate to Al Dhafra (UAE)
choices.flavor: Expensive but pragmatic. UAE welcomes the business. Qatar is relieved.
choices.consequence: You reinforce base defenses and relocate critical assets to dispersed positions, absorbing the attack while hardening your posture. The defensive response is paired with a diplomatic ultimatum delivered through Swiss intermediaries.

choices.label: Harden defenses and stay
choices.flavor: THAAD batteries deployed. The troops feel exposed. Morale drops.
choices.consequence: You disperse air operations across multiple bases in the region, reducing the vulnerability of any single installation. The logistical complexity increases dramatically, but the strategic resilience improves.

events.scene: Ballistic missiles are inbound toward Al Udeid Air Base in Qatar, the nerve center of US air operations in the Gulf. Patriot batteries fire in rapid sequence, streaks of light arcing into the night sky. Most of the missiles are intercepted, but fragments rain down on the flight line. One hangar is burning. The base commander reports casualties. This is the first direct Iranian attack on an American military installation.

events.title: INDIA ENERGY EMERGENCY
events.description: India declares a national energy emergency. They import 80% of their oil through the Strait. PM Modi is calling — demanding action or they will negotiate directly with Iran.

choices.label: Promise priority escort for Indian tankers
choices.flavor: Indian-flagged tankers get USN escorts. Modi is grateful. Other nations want the same deal.
choices.consequence: You authorize emergency oil shipments to India, diverting tankers from other routes and tapping strategic reserves. The gesture cements an alliance and eases the humanitarian crisis, though your own reserves dwindle further.

choices.label: Support India-Iran bilateral talks
choices.flavor: India opens a line to Tehran. Hawks accuse you of outsourcing diplomacy.
choices.consequence: You propose a joint Indian-American diplomatic initiative to reopen the Strait, leveraging India's unique position as a nation with ties to both Iran and the West. The partnership is novel and promising.

choices.label: Tell India to wait
choices.flavor: Modi goes public with his frustration. India begins rationing. Blame falls on you.
choices.consequence: You tell India that the crisis will end when Iran stands down, and that international pressure on Tehran is the fastest path to relief. The message is honest but cold. India begins exploring alternative energy partnerships with Russia.

events.scene: India's prime minister calls you directly, bypassing the usual diplomatic channels. The subcontinent is running out of oil. Power cuts are spreading, factories are shutting down, and street protests are erupting in Mumbai and Delhi. India imports eighty percent of its oil through the Strait of Hormuz. 'My country is dying slowly,' the prime minister says. 'I need you to fix this.'

events.title: MODERATE FACTION REACHES OUT
events.description: An encrypted message through Swiss channels: Iran's moderate faction is planning to sideline the IRGC hardliners. They need 72 hours — and a gesture of good faith.

choices.label: Pause operations for 72 hours
choices.flavor: Radio silence. The moderate faction moves. Three IRGC commanders are placed under house arrest. A breakthrough.
choices.consequence: You authorize covert support for the moderate faction, providing intelligence, communications equipment, and quiet assurances through intermediaries. The operation is the highest-stakes gamble of your career. If it succeeds, the crisis ends. If it fails, it deepens catastrophically.

choices.label: Give them intel on hardliner positions
choices.flavor: Sharing intelligence with a foreign faction. If this leaks, it's an international scandal. But it works.
choices.consequence: You provide limited intelligence support while maintaining plausible deniability, giving the moderates enough help to improve their odds without committing America to the outcome. The calibrated approach limits your exposure but also limits your influence.

choices.label: This is a trap — intensify operations
choices.flavor: You don't trust it. Operations continue. The moderate window closes.
choices.consequence: You refuse to get involved, judging that American interference in Iranian domestic politics will only discredit the moderates and unite the country against foreign meddling. The moderates are on their own.

events.scene: The intelligence arrives in fragments, pieced together from SIGINT intercepts and human sources in Tehran. A faction of Iranian moderates — military officers, business leaders, reformist politicians — is planning to move against the hardliners. They want to know if America will support a political transition. The request comes with a warning: if this fails, the hardliners will be more dangerous than ever.

events.title: DOWNED KC-135 CREW
events.description: The KC-135 tanker shot down on Day 1 — search teams have located 2 surviving crew members on an Iranian island. IRGC is closing in.

choices.label: SEAL team rescue — extract at all costs
choices.flavor: Spec ops infiltrate under cover of darkness. Both Americans rescued alive. The nation rallies.
choices.consequence: You authorize the rescue mission with full air cover, scrambling fighters to establish a protective perimeter around the crash site. The helicopters go in fast and low. Iranian fast boats turn back when they see the air cover. Your crew comes home.

choices.label: Negotiate through Oman
choices.flavor: Slow, agonizing negotiations. The crew becomes a bargaining chip. Families go on CNN.
choices.consequence: You attempt to coordinate with Iran through the maritime deconfliction channel, requesting safe passage for the rescue. The channel crackles with static and delay. Every minute of negotiation is a minute your crew spends in the water.

choices.label: Deny the crew exists — classify everything
choices.flavor: The cover-up doesn't hold. When the truth emerges, the fallout is devastating.
choices.consequence: You leak the crew's location and the IRGC's approach to international media, creating a global audience for whatever happens next. The spotlight forces Iran's hand — taking prisoners on live television is a different calculus than doing it in the dark.

events.scene: Your downed KC-135 crew has been located — alive, floating in life rafts in the Arabian Sea, sixty nautical miles from the Iranian coast. IRGC fast boats have been spotted heading toward the same coordinates. A Navy helicopter rescue team is thirty minutes out. The race is on, and the lives of four American servicemembers hang in the balance.

events.title: NATO FRACTURES
events.description: France breaks with the US — Macron calls the Iran strikes "disproportionate" and blocks NATO solidarity statement. Germany is wavering. The UK stands with you — for now.

choices.label: Bilateral with the UK — bypass NATO
choices.flavor: The "special relationship" holds. But NATO is weaker than it's been since Suez.
choices.consequence: You dismiss European criticism and press forward unilaterally, calculating that American military power does not require European permission. The alliance frays further, but your operational freedom is preserved.

choices.label: Offer Europe a seat at the negotiating table
choices.flavor: Macron gets his summit. NATO cohesion is preserved. The diplomatic path opens wider.
choices.consequence: You fly to Brussels for an emergency NATO summit, spending two days in bruising negotiations to rebuild consensus. The communique that emerges is weaker than you wanted but stronger than your critics expected.

choices.label: Publicly shame France
choices.flavor: "Freedom fries" is trending. The base loves it. Diplomats are horrified.
choices.consequence: You exploit the split, cutting bilateral deals with supportive nations while publicly shaming the dissenters. The approach is divisive by design, rewarding loyalty and punishing defection.

events.scene: The European Union is fracturing over the Iran crisis. France and Germany are calling for immediate negotiations and a suspension of military operations. Britain stands with you, but the Eastern European nations are distracted by their own security concerns. The transatlantic alliance, the bedrock of American foreign policy for eighty years, is cracking along the fault lines of the Strait of Hormuz.

events.title: HOSTAGE CRISIS DEEPENS
events.description: Three of the 12 detained crew members appear on Iranian state TV, reading prepared statements calling for America to "stop its aggression." Intelligence suggests they're being held at Evin Prison, not a military facility — a signal Iran wants to negotiate. The families are on CNN demanding action. A retired admiral is calling for a rescue operation on Fox News.

choices.label: Send a private envoy to Tehran
choices.flavor: A former diplomat boards a flight to Muscat, then Tehran. Total media blackout. If this leaks, it's a political disaster.
choices.consequence: You authorize sending a special envoy to Tehran, someone with enough stature to demand access to the hostages and enough credibility to negotiate their release. The envoy departs within hours, carrying your personal letter to the Iranian foreign minister.

choices.label: Authorize a rescue operation
choices.flavor: Delta Force operators begin mission planning. The risk is enormous. If it fails, you own it.
choices.consequence: You greenlight a rescue operation. SEAL Team Six has been training for this contingency since the crisis began. The plan is audacious — a night insertion by helicopter into hostile territory. The risks are astronomical, but so is the potential reward.

choices.label: Impose personal sanctions on IRGC commanders
choices.flavor: A measured response that satisfies nobody fully. The families are disappointed. Iran is unmoved.
choices.consequence: You increase diplomatic pressure through every available channel, mobilizing the Red Cross, the UN, and allied governments to demand humanitarian access. The multilateral approach is slower but builds an irresistible weight of international expectation.

events.scene: The hostage situation has deteriorated. Iran has moved the detained tanker crews from the port facility to an unknown location inland. Your intelligence has lost track of them. The families are on television, pleading for action. Congressional pressure is mounting. Time is working against you — every day the hostages spend in Iranian custody makes their recovery more difficult and more dangerous.

events.title: THE ENVOY REPORTS
events.description: Your envoy spent 72 hours in Tehran. The news is mixed. Iran will release 8 of the 12 crew — but they're keeping 4 "for investigation into espionage." The price: unfreeze $2 billion in Iranian assets and issue a joint statement about "mutual respect for maritime sovereignty." The envoy says the moderates were sincere but scared. The IRGC was listening to every word.

choices.label: Accept the partial release
choices.flavor: 8 crew members land at Ramstein. The families cry on camera. But 4 remain. The opposition calls it "paying ransom."
choices.consequence: You accept the partial release, calculating that getting some hostages home is worth the cost and that the precedent of negotiation will pave the way for the rest. The freed hostages arrive at Ramstein Air Base to cameras and tears.

choices.label: All 12 or no deal
choices.flavor: You draw the line. Iran goes silent. The 4 are moved to an unknown location.
choices.consequence: You reject the partial deal and demand all hostages be released, threatening escalation if Iran continues to hold American-affiliated civilians. The envoy delivers the ultimatum with a steady voice and sweating palms.

events.scene: Your envoy has been in Tehran for three days. The negotiations have been grueling — marathon sessions in windowless rooms, accusations and counteraccusations, moments of progress followed by hours of deadlock. Now the envoy calls with an update: Iran is willing to release some hostages in exchange for unfreezing certain assets. Not all the hostages. Not all the assets. A partial deal in a world where partial is the best you can get.

events.title: OPERATION EAGLE CLAW II
events.description: The rescue force launched at 0200 local time. Two MH-60 Black Hawks, 24 operators, supported by AC-130 gunships. They reached Evin Prison and breached the facility. But Iranian intelligence was waiting.

choices.label: Read the after-action report
choices.flavor: 3 operators KIA. 2 hostages killed in crossfire. 6 rescued. Iran parades the wreckage on TV. The world recoils. This is your Desert One.
choices.consequence: The rescue team fights its way out with casualties on both sides. No hostages are recovered. The mission is a failure by any measure — American servicemembers dead, hostages still captive, and Iran broadcasting footage of the abandoned equipment. You stare at the Situation Room screens and absorb the weight of what you have done.

events.scene: The rescue mission has launched. Black Hawks cross the Iranian coast at wave-top height, their rotors cutting the predawn darkness. The SEALs insert at the target compound under covering fire from AC-130 gunships. Then the operation goes sideways — the hostages have been moved, the compound is a trap, and your operators are taking fire from positions that were supposed to be empty. The extraction is chaotic, contested, and costly.

events.title: MOJTABA CONSOLIDATES
events.description: Mojtaba Khamenei has purged three senior IRGC commanders who opposed his succession. Satellite imagery shows Republican Guard units redeploying to Tehran — not to the Gulf. Intelligence suggests he's more afraid of internal rivals than of you. A CIA source inside the Guardian Council reports he might be open to a deal if it helps him consolidate power.

choices.label: Reach out through CIA channels
choices.flavor: Your message reaches Mojtaba through three cutouts. He doesn't respond — but he doesn't shut it down either.
choices.consequence: You open the direct channel, sending a trusted intelligence officer to a discreet meeting in a third country. The conversation is unprecedented — an American operative sitting across from the presumptive Supreme Leader's envoy, sketching the outlines of a new relationship on the back of a napkin.

choices.label: Exploit the chaos — increase pressure
choices.flavor: While Iran fights itself, you tighten the noose. Sanctions hit harder when the government is distracted.
choices.consequence: You exploit Mojtaba's vulnerability, tightening sanctions and increasing military pressure while he is weak. The gamble is that a cornered Mojtaba will make concessions his father never would.

choices.label: Publicly support Iranian moderates
choices.flavor: Your speech is played on BBC Persian. Iranian moderates are now accused of being American agents. You meant well.
choices.consequence: You share the intelligence about Mojtaba's overture with allies, building a multilateral approach to the succession while maintaining deniability about direct contact. The approach is cautious but risks losing the moment.

events.scene: Your intelligence assets in Tehran report that Mojtaba Khamenei has made his move. He has purged three IRGC generals and installed loyalists in key positions. But the power play has left him isolated — he needs external validation to consolidate. Your station chief in the region sends a flash cable: Mojtaba's people want to talk. Not through intermediaries. Directly.

events.title: THE SUPREME LEADER'S OFFER
events.description: Mojtaba's people made contact. The proposal is extraordinary: Iran will reopen the strait and halt enrichment above 20% — permanently. In exchange, they want recognition of the new Supreme Leader, removal of all personal sanctions on Mojtaba, and a secret $5 billion reconstruction fund. The CIA says he's genuine. The risk: if Congress finds out, it's Iran-Contra 2.0.

choices.label: Accept the grand bargain
choices.flavor: You make a deal with the devil you don't know. The strait opens. Oil flows. But the secret eats at you. One leak and it's over.
choices.consequence: You sign the secret deal, committing American resources and credibility to a man you have never met face-to-face. The implementation begins quietly — sanctions waivers processed through back doors, military stand-downs disguised as routine rotations. The deception is elaborate, dangerous, and potentially historic.

choices.label: Accept partially — strait for sanctions relief only
choices.flavor: Mojtaba gets less than he wanted but more than he had. The strait reopens gradually. Both sides claim victory.
choices.consequence: You agree to a scaled-down version of the deal, reducing the scope and the secrecy requirement. The partial agreement is less transformative but more sustainable, requiring fewer lies and fewer risks.

choices.label: Report to Congress and decline
choices.flavor: You do the right thing. Congress applauds your transparency. Iran's moderates collapse. Mojtaba becomes a hardliner overnight.
choices.consequence: You walk away, judging that a secret deal with an unproven leader is too fragile a foundation for lasting peace. You tell Mojtaba's envoy that America will negotiate publicly or not at all. The channel closes. The moment passes.

events.scene: The back channel with Mojtaba's faction has produced something extraordinary — a draft agreement that would fundamentally reshape Iran's relationship with the West. Sanctions relief, nuclear limits, Gulf security guarantees, proxy force drawdowns. It is the grand bargain that every administration since the Revolution has dreamed of and none has achieved. The catch: Mojtaba needs the deal to remain secret until he has fully consolidated power. One leak, and the hardliners will tear it apart — and possibly tear Mojtaba apart with it.

events.title: IAEA DEMANDS ACCESS
events.description: Following your push for international inspection, the IAEA team arrived at Fordow — and was turned away. Iran claims "construction" prevents access. Satellite imagery shows new tunneling activity. The IAEA chief calls you directly: "They're 2-3 weeks from enough material for a device. Maybe less." Israel is moving Jericho missiles to launch positions.

choices.label: Coordinate strikes with Israel on Fordow
choices.flavor: Bunker busters penetrate 80 meters of rock. Fordow is destroyed. Iran vows revenge "for a thousand years." The nuclear program is set back 5 years — or 5 months.
choices.consequence: You authorize the strike on Fordow, ordering bunker-busting munitions to destroy what the inspectors cannot verify. The mountain shudders under the impact. The enrichment facility is buried deeper, this time under rubble. The world enters uncharted territory.

choices.label: Threaten strikes unless Iran admits inspectors
choices.flavor: An ultimatum with teeth. The world watches.
choices.consequence: You present Iran with a Security Council ultimatum backed by your intelligence package. Forty-eight hours to grant full access, or face comprehensive sanctions and military consequences. The clock starts now. Iran's ambassador at the UN shifts uncomfortably in his chair.

choices.label: Propose a new deal — enrichment caps for sanctions relief
choices.flavor: Critics call it "JCPOA 2.0." Supporters call it "the only path that doesn't end in mushroom clouds."
choices.consequence: You propose a face-saving compromise — a limited inspection of specific areas with a timeline for full access. The arrangement gives Iran a graceful retreat and gives you verification, even if imperfect. Your hawks call it capitulation. Your diplomats call it the only move that avoids war.

events.scene: The IAEA inspection team has arrived at Fordow — and been denied access to the deep underground halls. The Iranian military has surrounded the facility with troops and declared the inspection 'suspended for security reasons.' Your inspectors are confined to a guest house while satellites show centrifuge halls operating at full capacity. The verification regime you staked your credibility on is collapsing in real time.

events.title: IRAN'S 72-HOUR RESPONSE
events.description: The deadline passes. Iran's response comes not through diplomats but through actions: they move centrifuge cascades to a new, unknown facility. Meanwhile, Mojtaba appears on television and says, "Iran will never bow to nuclear blackmail — we seek peaceful energy, and we will defend our rights." The IAEA says they've lost track of 8kg of enriched uranium.

choices.label: Launch strikes on all known nuclear sites
choices.flavor: Operation Olympic Games. B-2s from Whiteman, Tomahawks from the Gulf. 47 targets in one night. The region will never be the same.
choices.consequence: You call the bluff and launch strikes against Iran's nuclear infrastructure. Fordow, Natanz, Isfahan — the entire fuel cycle hit simultaneously in a single night of precision bombing. The mushroom clouds are conventional, but the strategic landscape has shifted irreversibly.

choices.label: Accept the new reality — contain and deter
choices.flavor: You choose containment over war. History will judge whether this was wisdom or cowardice.
choices.consequence: You blink. The carriers remain, but the ultimatum dies quietly in classified cables. Iran keeps its centrifuges. You keep your carriers. The status quo holds, but everyone knows who flinched.

events.scene: Iran's response to your nuclear ultimatum arrives at the stroke of the deadline. It is not a capitulation. It is not a rejection. It is a counter-ultimatum: withdraw your carrier groups from the Gulf within seventy-two hours, or Iran will expel all remaining international monitors and accelerate enrichment to weapons grade. The Supreme Leader appears on state television, flanked by generals, calling your bluff. The world has never been closer to a nuclear-armed Iran.

events.title: [ IMMINENT THREAT ]
events.description: Multiple intelligence agencies confirm a credible, imminent assassination plot. You have minutes to decide.

choices.label: Full lockdown — cancel all public appearances
choices.flavor: You go underground. The threat is neutralized but you look weak.

choices.label: Counter-strike on the plotters
choices.flavor: Special forces neutralize the cell. The message is clear.

choices.label: Ignore it — show strength
choices.flavor: You appear in public defiantly. The risk remains.

events.title: [ NUCLEAR THRESHOLD ]
events.description: IAEA reports Iran at 90% enrichment with 440kg stockpile. Israel is on the phone. "We strike in 12 hours unless you act." Russia warns any strike will be treated as aggression against their ally.

choices.label: Green-light Israeli strike
choices.flavor: Israeli F-35s hit Fordow and Natanz. Iran vows "devastating retaliation." Russia recalls ambassador. The nuclear clock resets but the war clock accelerates.
choices.consequence: You order an immediate strike on Fordow and all associated nuclear facilities. The B-2s are already airborne from Diego Garcia, carrying the thirty-thousand-pound Massive Ordnance Penetrators that were built for this exact scenario. You are about to bomb a nation's nuclear program. The last president who considered this chose not to.

choices.label: US strike — bunker busters on Fordow
choices.flavor: B-2 bombers drop GBU-57 MOPs. The mountain facility is destroyed. You own this escalation now.
choices.consequence: You authorize a broader military campaign — not just the nuclear sites but Iran's military infrastructure, command and control, air defenses. If you are going to war, you reason, do it once and do it completely. The generals nod. They have been planning this for decades.

choices.label: Emergency UN session — buy time
choices.flavor: The Security Council convenes. Israel fumes. Iran enriches. You bought 72 hours — at most.
choices.consequence: You take the intelligence to the UN Security Council, demanding emergency sanctions and international action. The diplomatic route feels inadequate against the ticking clock, but you refuse to be the president who started a war on the strength of intelligence that might be wrong.

choices.label: Call Tehran directly
choices.flavor: A stunning phone call. Iran agrees to freeze enrichment for 30 days. Hawks call it "the worst deal since Munich." But the bombs stay in their bays.
choices.consequence: You open an emergency back channel to Tehran, offering a comprehensive deal: sanctions relief, security guarantees, diplomatic recognition — everything on the table — in exchange for verifiable dismantlement. It is the most generous offer any American president has ever made to Iran, and it tastes like surrender.

choices.label: Do nothing — call the bluff
choices.flavor: Israel strikes alone 11 hours later. The fallout — literal and political — is now your problem without the credit.
choices.consequence: You do nothing visible. You increase surveillance, position assets, prepare contingencies, and wait for Iran to make its next move. The strategic patience is agonizing, but you refuse to let Tehran's timeline dictate your actions.

events.scene: The red phone rings at three in the morning. The CIA Director's voice is flat, drained of emotion, delivering the words that every president since 1979 has dreaded. Iran has crossed the nuclear threshold. Fordow's centrifuges have produced enough highly enriched uranium for a weapon. Weaponization timelines are uncertain — weeks, perhaps days. The Situation Room fills with people who have not slept, staring at satellite imagery of a mountain that now contains the end of the world as they know it.

events.title: [ FRIENDLY FIRE INCIDENT ]
events.description: USS Vella Gulf (CG-72) has engaged and destroyed a vessel in the strait. It was an Emirati coast guard cutter. 11 UAE personnel killed. The UAE ambassador is on the phone. Al Jazeera has the footage.

choices.label: Immediate public apology + compensation
choices.flavor: You own it immediately. $500M compensation. The UAE accepts but Al Udeid base access is "under review." Trust is damaged.
choices.consequence: You immediately acknowledge the incident and take full responsibility, ordering a stand-down of naval operations pending investigation. The President calls the Bahraini king personally. The apology is abject and sincere. The damage to the alliance will take years to repair.

choices.label: Blame fog of war — express regret
choices.flavor: "In the fog of war, terrible mistakes happen." The UAE pulls their ambassador. Gulf coalition fractures.
choices.consequence: You express condolences but frame the incident as an inevitable consequence of operating in a high-threat environment, shifting some blame to the fog of war and inadequate coordination. The Bahraini government sees through the deflection immediately.

choices.label: Classify the incident — suppress footage
choices.flavor: The cover-up lasts 36 hours before a sailor leaks the bridge recording. Now it's a scandal AND a tragedy.
choices.consequence: You attempt to suppress the details, classifying the investigation and pressuring social media platforms to remove the footage. The cover-up fails within twenty-four hours, compounding the original tragedy with a crisis of credibility.

choices.label: Stand down all operations for 48 hours
choices.flavor: A full operational pause. Iran exploits the gap. But no more mistakes. The Navy resets its ROE.
choices.consequence: You propose joint rules of engagement with all allied navies in the theater, turning the tragedy into a catalyst for better coordination. The initiative cannot undo the dead, but it might prevent the next friendly-fire incident.

events.scene: The worst has happened. In the fog of a nighttime naval engagement, an American destroyer has fired on and struck a vessel that turns out to be a Bahraini coast guard cutter operating in the same waters. Casualties are significant. The Bahraini government is in shock — they are your ally, their base hosts your fleet, and your weapons have just killed their sailors. The incident is already on social media, uploaded by a fishing boat that witnessed the explosion.

events.title: [ CASCADE FAILURE ]
events.description: Houthi anti-ship missile hits LNG tanker in Bab el-Mandeb. Tanker is on fire, crew abandoning ship. Simultaneously, IRGC mines detonate under a VLCC in Hormuz. AND Iraqi militias are rocketing Al Asad. Three theaters. One response.

choices.label: Prioritize Hormuz — it's the jugular
choices.flavor: You save the VLCC crew and clear the mines. The LNG tanker burns. Al Asad takes hits. You can't be everywhere.
choices.consequence: You prioritize the Strait, ordering all available assets to protect the tanker convoy while accepting risk on the other fronts. The choice is agonizing — you are abandoning allies and bases to focus on the economic chokepoint. Houthi missiles and Hezbollah rockets will fall unanswered.

choices.label: Retaliate everywhere — show no weakness
choices.flavor: Tomahawks hit all three fronts simultaneously. Iran's proxy network is degraded. Your budget is cratered. The region is on fire.
choices.consequence: You escalate to a full regional war footing, authorizing strikes against all three threat axes simultaneously. The American military machine shifts into high gear — every carrier, every bomber, every special operations unit in the region receives a target package. This is the war nobody wanted.

choices.label: Call emergency NATO Article 5
choices.flavor: First Article 5 invocation since 9/11. NATO responds — eventually. The bureaucracy buys Iran 72 hours to dig in.
choices.consequence: You reach for the diplomatic toolkit, proposing an emergency ceasefire through the UN while your forces play defense. The diplomatic gambit is a long shot, but the alternative is a regional conflagration that could burn for years.

choices.label: Emergency ceasefire offer to Iran
choices.flavor: You offer to stop all strikes if Iran reins in proxies. Iran says yes. Then the Houthis fire again the next day. "They don't control their own proxies."
choices.consequence: You address the nation, acknowledging the severity of the crisis and calling for calm. Behind the scenes, you authorize only defensive operations, refusing to be the one who escalates a bad situation into an irreversible catastrophe.

events.scene: Everything is happening at once. Houthi missiles are streaking toward Red Sea shipping. Hezbollah rockets are hitting targets in Iraq. An IRGC fast-boat swarm is converging on a tanker convoy in the Strait. Your forces are engaged on three fronts simultaneously, and the situation board looks like a war that has already started without anyone formally declaring it. Your theater commander is requesting reinforcements he knows you do not have. The cascade has begun.
